I love arts, culture, and fashion! There is so much to chew on in this sequel that it makes me question the function of its predecessor. That said, it could not exist without its maker. Not only are all the nods enjoyable without being over the top (a rare feat in a major sequel), but the characters feel even more developed, their lives lived in. There’s a richness to how the film depicts the impact of technology and capitalism on the creative world while also balancing the general impact of time passing and societal change. Nobody works better with this material than Meryl; this Miranda is both familiar and off her center as we know her and that doesn’t really change over the course of the film. And isn’t that what it’s like to lead in a dying industry right now.

Splitsville, but more violent and less funny (and less of a grounded drama, to be fair). Still, laugh out loud hilarious with smartly used plot devices. I need a whole movie just focused on Timothy Olyphant’s and Juliette Lewis’s characters meeting.

There’s so much to say about the fact that so little was said. Lowkey traumatizing, this is what happens when you let Quentin Tarantino be too close to Blackness. The vibes were student film, no shade.
Needed sharper, smarter storytelling. Gigi was massively underutilized.
That was basically a nostalgiacore acid trip. Mind you I had never seen this before…

A very transparent documentary in that it conveyed how generally horny this man’s legacy is. Or maybe that’s just my affinity for John Mulaney. Either way, had a surprisingly emotional time in the theater by myself. Makes me question if I should care more about SNL (still no, I think?). Lorne is undoubtedly a singular legend.
I lowkey needed more of the witch. Justice for Jerry!
We’ve lost the art of romcom scripting. How much did Italy’s tourism board invest?

I have to imagine the morality play at the center of this read more when it first came out. Now, it’s impossible to look away from the broader cautionary tale. In either respect, the film never really drives home the point effectively. The set design was really enjoyable, though. And I was surprisingly captivated by the dance sequence.

I think this would’ve worked much better as a stage play (not musical). There’s a richness to the intimacy and ethereal plot that is amazing to witness, but doesn’t feel quite right for the medium. Jack’s influence was felt (derogatory).
I get that we’re telling a story, but these people are just bad at following instructions and collaborating with children. Had a great time puzzle solving with them each round, though.
Every element is just plot fodder, which is fine for a film like this. Was there a lowkey pro-trans narrative or am I that one friend that’s too woke?

Curious that a film about two artists, great in their own right, never quite becomes great itself. It feels like we just kiss the most interesting nodes. Where is the three-hander version of this with Owen? Ian McKellen maybe never hotter, though.

I have not seen the original, but I appreciate that this is explicitly referential, rather than a pure remake. That said, it’s unsure if it’s commenting on social media’s impact on our perceptions of content or being a campy sendup of the original, given how it would play in today’s world. The tonal shifting throughout results in neither being particularly effective. While Barbie and Dacre give enjoyable performances, they don’t deliver the gravitas to accomplish the former nor the commitment to nail the latter.

tmdb-1641961.title: Untold: The Death & Life of Lamar Odom

reviews.reviewText: Khloé is stronger than me.

If you are going into this thinking it’s a straightforward romcom, that’s on you. Media literacy is dying and I think many will miss the point of this one. That said, I loved the darkly comedic exploration of morals and ethics, suitable for the Boston setting. The politic of race, gender, and woke-ism is made so tangible and the break into the film’s third act really nails how all of that comes together. Honestly, a great case for being single. Hailey Benton Gates always steals the scene.

tmdb-1596335.title: The AI Doc: Or How I Became an Apocaloptimist

reviews.reviewText: Lowkey irresponsibly made. The future is bright and bleak. May we all find our peace.
It’s important to try new things, but somewhere in here it became focused on Jason Sudeikis as an abusive protagonist and never figured out how to properly handle him as a sidekick turned antagonist.

tmdb-1573688.title: Color Theories by Julio Torres. A Guide to Seeing the World

reviews.reviewText: I want to live in Julio’s piscean dreamworld so bad! This felt a bit rushed, but I also think it knows that. I could’ve watched this for hours. Consider this my petition for a Color Theories show (preferably on PBS).
Looked great, but too much time spent in the world of realism to then upend everything in the last 5 minutes. If we’re going fantastical, let’s get to it!
Victoria Pedretti got to work! Fun and flirty throughout. I’m obsessed that this takes place entirely in and around a mall. I’m shaking at the idea of a Gabrielle Union-driven sequel.
I wish the whole thing had been focused on the child army training in a fake Swiss town scenario.
Another great romp, but I don’t believe for a second that this is where the story ends.
I like that they just took the story to the action without trying too hard. Also obsessed with everyone using his full name.
Ruby Rose’s presence, a dark harbinger.
Single people having to save the world, yet again!
Honestly, an incredibly rational response. We deserved one “do svidaniya,” though.
Had a great time, but there’s not much in here beyond the violent action sequences. The pig head of it all…

tmdb-1266127.title: Ready or Not 2: Here I Come

reviews.reviewText: This was fun! I love that they pick up right after the prior entry. I feel like the sister relationship wasn’t fully fleshed out, but whatever it takes to get Shawn Hatosy on the big screen. If the release timing of the first movie indicates the present day timeline, I love the implication that Chester was watching the 2019 US Open while controlling an armed conflict.

Obsessed with Angie MacDowell in this. Fun, but I wish they had made use of the billionaire house/Clue aesthetics. Mostly watched this to have an excuse to see Shawn Hatosy on the big screen in the sequel.
Was this anti-abortion propaganda or am I losing it?

I think the allegory is a little heavy-handed and the script could’ve been cut a bit. Beyond that, there’s a richness to the storytelling and handling of time and memory. Ultimately, I’m not sure that this is saying as much as it thinks it is, though.

Touching, but the BAFTAs controversy was in the back of my head the whole time. The picture this film paints makes it terribly clear how John Davidson was and continues to be enabled to be an unapologetic asshole who uses his disability as a shield from accountability.
I want to be Claudio Giombi when I grow up!
Shallow doc, but Diane is that girl! It’s too bad her autistic focus is an award that has come to overshadow her artistry. In a way, manifesting a comeback for her.

tmdb-682012.title: Little Amélie or the Character of Rain

reviews.reviewText: Wait, I really thought she was God that whole time…
Even more troubling when you consider the American curriculum of the late ‘90s, at least, onward.

The parts are all there, but the assembly is shoddy. I honestly don’t even fully know how to feel. I definitely think a campy Halloween audience would’ve made this more enjoyable in the moment, rather than confusing on a random March afternoon.

tmdb-848331.title: Honk for Jesus. Save Your Soul.

reviews.reviewText: What a ride. Blistering, hilarious, and often triggering, this film was like looking in the rearview mirror and seeing the culture I grew up in. The story is driven much more by what isn’t said than what it is, and it’s Regina Hall’s performance that drives it all home. I wonder if people from this type of church community have seen this and if they’re able to see the truth in the fiction.

tmdb-1644807.title: Harry Styles. One Night in Manchester.

reviews.reviewText: A straightforward event film, but really enjoyed the set design. Ultimately, just really happy to have this guy back.
A film has not angered me so much (complimentary) in a while. Sara is a true g!

The rare instance of a long film that I want more time with. I could stand to see this adapted into a miniseries, just to examine the layered storylines a bit more. Beautiful and heartbreaking throughout, and also both rough and tender. I wish I had gotten to see this in IMAX.

Powerful, contemplative, and gripping until the last 15 minutes undoes all the progression. Realistic, sure. But also so pessimistic about who we can become and our propensity for change in our later stages of life. Not my ministry, but my Lesley Manville stocks continue to outperform!
She lowkey irked me.

I’ve been thinking so much about time, and life, and age, and death lately, so this spoke right to me. My eyes were wet the entire time. Such a beautifully simplistic story and animation for topics with such complexity.
Beautiful stylistically, but a bit slow. A touching tribute, all the same.
Interesting style, but not for me.
Nah — actually fuck this greedy bear!
Big daddy sailor was controlling it, chile!
Funny down, but so cheesy and on the nose.
I felt like I watched a whole feature. There’s such a rich and lived in — and weird, bizarre, dystopian (???) — world in this.
Punch a Zionist tuh-day!
I love Miriam Margoryles and I love British theatre.
Pretty much just a vehicle to showcase amazing vocal/musical talent. And I love that. Many of my favorite songs mentioned. Cute plot to boot.
The writer at least hates women, but I suspect hates Black women specifically. Killer had a hot body, though.

tmdb-1560400.title: Children No More: Were and Are Gone

reviews.reviewText: Racism and Zionism are so intertwined and I found myself gesticulating at the screen like I would change any of these people’s minds.
Quietly disturbing. When will we wake up? Anyone justifying lack of gun control needs to reflect.

tmdb-1422051.title: Armed Only with a Camera: The Life and Death of Brent Renaud

reviews.reviewText: Somewhat self-indulgent, even posthumously. Journalism is under attack, but likening it to the horrors they’re reporting on…not quite!
Timely, upsetting, and saddening.
Looks and sounds amazing, but not much to latch onto. I guess…donkeys…
I mean…I love miniatures. And that goes for Gael, too.
A great time, but some serious narrative issues that caused the plot to feel bloated, rather than full. However, they could never make me hate Barry and I knew it was Jennifer before I saw her.

Not having read the book or seen prior productions, I have to imagine they nailed their particular angle. I think the trouble is that melodrama is hard to sell in high definition. That said, execution is not really the problem as much as the market. Needed more moments of the score intersecting with Charli’s soundtrack. Isabella forever.
A fun time if not massively unserious. Sosie Bacon kinda gave Gina Rodriguez teas.

tmdb-1400780.title: Come See Me in the Good Light

reviews.reviewText: Familiar and heartbreaking.

I think there is something about how both Glen Powell and Margaret Qualley affect content in a way that often highlights the line between a great movie and an okay film that aspires to be more. I’m not mad at that, but it’s a consistent observation.
A necessary, but altogether unsurprising, film. Harrowing and nauseating, yet unfortunately not shocking.
Not my taste, but well-executed. I was impressed by the blending of animation styles and the music composition.
Depressed/traumatized kid is the funniest in the room representation.
I was put off by the gargoyles and the Wonka wig, but I’m glad Christoph Waltz showed up to put in work.
More curious about the infighting within the British intelligence community, but sure.
The Masked Singer mentioned. This was for the angels.
Great concept, but lacking a proper conclusion. It almost would’ve been better with no dialogue. Need a dance screening immediately, to be fair.
Lowkenuinely depressing. Alexander Skarsgård is always gonna nail his own tone and it meshes so well with the tone of this script. I laughed, I cried, I was turned on — what more could one ask for?
Maybe my favorite animation style with a quirky script/dialogue that complements the visual feast. Somehow I want to move to Florida and give life a try.
This was really cute and enjoyable. I’m rooting for Clifford!

Even knowing what’s coming, it was so difficult to watch. Certainly looking away was not on the table. The intersplicing of real audio and footage put me in a place. I cannot believe we live in a timeline where this level of criminal activity is witnessed and we do nothing about it. Sick and twisted people.
The ending felt a but telegraphed for me, but the tension buildup over the course of the movie was top notch.
I want a truly villainous snake, even though I appreciate the subversion. So many actors whose voices I love in this one.
Strong copaganda because I was laughing the whole time. Jason Bateman’s voice is powerful because I started to think this fix looks like him.
Literally traumatizing.
Triangle of Sadness nachos. Loved the Mean Girls references.

tmdb-1119449.title: Good Luck, Have Fun, Don't Die

reviews.reviewText: Maybe it’s because I’m Pluribus-pilled, but I loved every minute of that. I guess I’m a Haley Lu Richardson stan now…
At one point, Ben’s waist was very cunty. Otherwise, nothing to write home about.
IJBOL. Not a shred of irony.

Talk about letting it rip. Beautiful and tragic. Kristen Stewart came out swinging in her directorial debut. Imogen Poots made me so sick (complimentary). The editors got down. I do think men should all be killed and women would be more than justified to do it.
Looks more interesting than the actual story is, but I’m glad to have finally seen it. The church scene took a lot out of me.
Perfect for a rain day on the couch (high compliment). I could watch Jodie Foster read the phone book.
I don’t think it quite hits the philosophical notes, but it was a riot to watch and I’m definitely curious about the next entry. “How’s that?” is entering my lexicon, I fear.
Three of my favorite baddies in a bad movie. Fun in its ridiculousness.
Silly Caucasian family likes to play with chimpanzees. Needed more Troy Kotsur.
I quite liked how weird this was! It took a while to get going, but I continue to realize I have not been paying as much attention to Andrew Garfield as I should be.

I think this should’ve been left as a one and done. All this hemming and hawing about climate change without really dealing with how whiteness has created the mess in this franchise. And then literally killing all the Black characters before they’re even developed. Meanwhile, we’re using creative license to make Nathan 15?
They really ripped it in the final act. I love how it shifts from almost a political drama to a danced parable. There are Viewpoints everywhere for those with eyes to see.

I prefer highbrow over lowbrow satire. But the biggest issue with this film is that it can’t decide which it is. The resulting note is bland, but sour. Everyone involved both overcommitted and undercommitted. I’d love to have seen this after a serious script edit.
I’m shocked at how bored I was. Benedict Cumberbatch with the bayang…

HAL terrifies me. Love the use of silence throughout, but the long cuts leading to an intermission are proof that this could’ve used a bit more editing. A film to watch if you care more about visuals than story, to be honest.

Outstanding performances and overall story, but there’s a missing urgency in the script and dialogue. If my sister had been missing for two years, our reunion simply wouldn’t be this casual and without more immediate professional assistance.
One of those situations where you can see where it could be good, but all the elements never quite come together. It’s always nice to see Janina Gavankar.
Sad porn to start the new year.

An auspicious final film of the year as I sit alone on my couch. Lanthimos builds a really interesting world in the first half, but it doesn’t hold up much on the back end. I think I’d like to become a whale.
Melodramatic, and also flat. It’s not bad, as much as it’s not good.

I understand the choice in storytelling approach is a certain constraint, but I don’t care about anyone enough to care what was going on outside of the raw action. I feel like there’s so much going on just for this to not say anything.
I could be missing something, but I doubt it. So directionless that you either love it or, like me, are ambivalent toward it. Always nice seeing Jake, though.

I maybe hyped this one up for myself. I ended up thinking it was just fine, but I was hoping for some more Massachusetts vibes that I could identify. Parenting is getting more stressful by the day and I’m becoming more averse to it, the more content like this I see.

I do love a climate horror film. This was a fine showing, but for its runtime I wish it had posed more questions about how humanity ends up in this situation, rather than just accepting it and asking how people behave as usual. There were so many entry points set up for that, but it feels like none of them were exploited.

Deeply triggering and it didn’t help that it was set in the year when I was this same age. Boys are terrorists. I was crawling out of my skin as much as I was laughing at Kayo Martin’s deliveries. And to be honest, that’s exactly the problem with Jakes — I’ve know more than a few and he really nails that. When he literally wiped the smirk of his face? It’s an art form.
Not my taste, doesn’t hold up well, and a deeply triggering reminder of my liberal arts college education. Maybe this is a documentary…

There was a woman in my audience commenting at every plot point, which would make you think we were watching a thriller or at least a more interesting drama. At the end of the day, it’s a soapy little melancholy holiday rom com musical. And knows that. So much emotional labor put on the women in this narrative while trying to frame them as the most emotionally demanding. This is one of those films that has the trapping of liberalism, but lowkey is a hair’s breadth from giving fully alt right vibes. I wonder if/when Joey Morof will start singing Neil Diamond songs, but also…as 24601.
I guess I enjoyed the British vibes and the final fight sequences.
I need to talk to a Black person! This was so funny and enjoyable. And honestly, I would watch a few sequels for more hijinks.
Like many, I love anything spy-related. I am shocked at how bored I was watching this.
It’s simple: believe women. And also, Julia must’ve really been in love because her man is lowkey trash.
Cate as a fake chef on a motorcycle is so healing to see.
The whole things feels like a dream sequence. Soft and meandering if that’s your thing.

I wasn’t expecting a musical, but I love the exploration of ritual through sound and movement, as much as this film is also an exploration of religion and gender. Amanda Seyfried keeps turning in career best work and nobody is ever loud enough about it for me. Haunting and heart-wrenching, I’ll be thinking of this one for a while.
I’m okay with this one not being for me. Like why was Tom Cruise’s hair that color?

Honestly, mildly surprised at how bored I was. I didn’t expect to love it, but this is a low for Baz. There seems to be a film, no pun intended, over his usual take on opulence. Everything feels dull and almost inconsequential. And with low stakes, the work can’t help but suffer.

This wanted to be Alien so bad. Aaron Paul’s talent was incredibly wasted and, in the end, I’m not even certain what greater point this was trying to make because the main character was the villain all along to me, still.
Looks great, especially if you ignore the foundation of the series.
Cute, but not life-changing.
A little gimmicky for my taste, but Denzel and Ethan sure do act the shit out of it. Kind of a whack ending though — Black on Black crime…
A well-acted, but shallow exploration of the dynamics that propelled the Williams sisters into success. I just know Aunjanue don’t play about Jon Bernthal.
Sometimes we just need to put on something silly and cute. I want to flirt with Kumail next!
I’m not sure that we needed this, but Ewan McGregor was great and Rebecca Ferguson was…in a top hat.
A bit flat for my liking. The stakes never feel as high as they should be and the tension doesn’t seem to show up until the last 20 minutes. But hey, Allison Janney!

Looked great and the sound editing was strong, but overall this film doesn’t really say much. Of course Colin Farrell was great, but Tilda was underused. Ultimately, maybe this was just an excuse for sweeping shots of the Vegas of the East.
Perfect for a lazy Sunday where you don’t need to consume anything with gravitas.
John Travolta doing his best Nic Cage. But to a much lesser effect.

Chaotic and messy — just the way I like it. I forgot how good Will Arnett is and the rest of the cast really showed up, especially Andra Day. Laura Dern is obviously great, but we have to stop saying talented women are “a revelation.” Ultimately, this would’ve been rated higher if the third act didn’t seem so rushed as to be almost nonexistent.
Chaos in both content and production. Everyone pissed me off and Kirby and Jason Leigh couldn’t even save it.
I was coerced into finally seeing this. And I don’t need it to happen ever again, but my friend wants to screen it annually. Note to self: more drugs next time.

Chalamet finally cracked the code! What a tour de force. I was locked in the entire time—even when you know how it’s all going to go, there is still a command of interest as to what the journey to the conclusion looks like. Marty Mauser, MM, flip it around…
At the end of the day, I love George Michael and this song so much. What is Mary doing here?
Not good, but not bad. Definitely an interesting plot and somewhat of an interesting twist, but enough interesting content overall.

Better than Bigelow’s prior entry, but feels like it goes out of its way to glorify the US’s war tactics. Ironically, more acceptable during an Obama presidency, but certainly not aging well. At the very least the covert war action and political drama plots were not well-enmeshed.
A little style over substance, but man we don’t make ‘em like this anymore. Jack Nicholson shouldn’t be so hot? Significant points lost for the multiple hard Rs.
These boys hate each other? And then they are dying? Kiefer Sutherland been a baddie, though…

So bizarrely chaotic that it almost becomes good. Too many plot lines and not vibing on the narrative for our girl. Men ain’t shit and she should’ve killed every one in this movie, save for her brother.

I’m not a huge musical person, but everyone brought their A game to this. In particular, Katie Rose Clarke delivers a knockout performance with a touch of Glinda. Of note, I will say that this is not a movie musical, so I expected to see more of the staging; the viewing experience felt a bit off, relative to the content.

I was anxious the entire time. Wild to believe they released this only 7 years after 9/11. Stress does not make a great movie experience, though. I had to distract myself with other things the whole time.

The stress of hospitality work is definitely captured. Just about everyone is an asshole and that’s exactly how it is! I’ve never worked in an open kitchen, but that added element seemed rather unused, and the timing of the events in the shift seem accelerated to an unnatural pace just to fit into the one-take. Why was the racist table, of all things, never really addressed…
I never felt locked in on this one. I think the different elements just felt uncoordinated to the point that I don’t know how to critique this better.

I could’ve watched this for several more hours. Beautifully done and an amazing tribute to the visual language. I couldn’t look away and I had tears in my eyes. A good reminder of how risk and reward can meet in the film space.
Didn’t know Jesse Eisenberg had a red-pilled closeted bottom murderer in him, but okay.
Heart-wrenching and terrifying. We simply need Lucy Liu in more work!

I was enamored the entire way through. For starters, I love an action-y film that gets right to it. Bill Skarsgard’s ability to rapidly shift back and forth between tones and create mini beats is on full display. Dacre Montgomery also proving why he might be one of the best talents to come out of Stranger Things. And the overall ensemble is used incredibly well, painting quite the picture of calm among the chaos.
Let’s let Unbreakable be the star here. Always chilling when the scapegoat villain is named Malcolm and the real villain is a child.
Having recently been on my first cruise, I can say there are more dramatic and interesting things going on than are in this plot. Not enough Hannah Waddingham or Gugu Mbatha-Raw for me.
Truly iconic front to back — pun intended. Especially here for the campy tone and line readings. Everyone understood the assignment.
Not the most compelling space narrative I’ve seen, but not bad either. At the very least, Mars looks gorgina.
I famously fight sleep in many a movie; I was not motivated to put up much of a fight for this one.
General dystopian narrative with little new to say.
At the end of the day, men are so fucking weak. Not the most captivating way I’ve seen this story told, but interesting concept nonetheless.

So incredibly dark and funny. It hit a little too close at home for this unemployed viewer, but wow did I love how it just kept becoming more and more unhinged. Because that it is how it feels in this market. Lee Byung-hun is everything, as always.

tmdb-414419.title: Kill Bill: The Whole Bloody Affair

reviews.reviewText: I had never seen these in theaters and that was nice. Seeing them this way, though, emphasises the issue I’ve always had — Vol. 1 is buoyed by Lucy Liu and the character of O-Ren (and those surrounding her). The additional anime sequence’s compelling nature affirms this, even though it’s also extraneous. The precipitous falloff of driving pathos in Vol. 2 is palpable from the minute the intermission ends. And it’s not to say that the second installment is bad, just that it takes a rather hard left tonally that I’ve never preferred. It feels like I’m watching two standalone movies, more than an original and a sequel. And if Tarantino always intended they be together, then I fear he should’ve edited them into a singular film with a longer runtime.
Moving and frequently funny, but many character explorations wind up shallow, despite the lengthy runtime. The son and researcher’s relationship was my favorite. And, of course, Dona Sebastiana.
For the love of all that is holy, can we please stop making films designed just to sell a sequel? Anyways, hello to Josh Hutcherson and Matthew Lillard still.
Love the aesthetics and story, but we just didn’t really say anything.

We went style over substance and Charli ended up giving a better performance than I was expecting. Of course the narrative of women’s agency, particularly to pursue literacy, is compelling but there’s really no meaningful conflict or fully threatened consequence to anyone involved. Nicholas Galitzine continues to prove he’s a little freak.

Josh O’Connor reminds us of the true importance of being earnest. Rian Johnson deployed this ensemble expertly and meaningfully asks us to think about faith and community while watching a murder mystery. I’m looking forward to marathoning this series.

Men will make a movie named Jay Kelly, about a man named Jay Kelly who will make a movie before going to therapy, before going to therapy. The best part of this was various women opting out of the bullshit.
Very much so giving flat Sprite.
Interesting idea, but I simply never would’ve taken this drug that many times.

One of the more interesting elements of this film is the natural horror, witchcraft, and religion angle. If you’re not in it for anything else, that will grab you and draw you in. Buckley and Mescal obviously give outstanding performances; Mescal, in particular, has what many (not all) of his peers are after. The theatre nerds will also love this, especially if they’ve ever deeply studied Shakespearean text. All in all, a tender and moving reflection on the life of this family, but Max Richter will do time for his recycling of the final needle drop.
Not enough tennis for its title to be what it is. Watched this against my own will and better judgment, even though I was also the person that pressed play.
Candice Bergen the only one making sense in this whole thing because Patrick Dempsey deserved better. Like how are you leaving that man at the altar?
A lot going on in here that I like, especially John Boyega 😮‍💨 Teyonah Parris was the true lead, in my opinion. I have mixed feeling about the Kiefer jump scare.
The poster is better than the actual film. The overall production made little sense for the story and I was very much locked OUT.
Really pretty, but it feels like we don’t get to any substance until halfway through the final act.
I’m so emotional! Everyone in here is going through a lot. I’m shocked that I came around to the Principal, in the end. Young love must be protected at all costs!!
This felt like two movies slapped together. Neither of those movies is bad, but they’re not brought together cohesively into a singular narrative. Fuck the carceral state.
Bad, and also a pre-documentary.
I love these two little cuties and their friendship so much! Even in the end, we deserve love. And while I get it…they totally should’ve kissed.
Men are so fucking evil. It makes me sick! Kill them all!!!
Okay, this was cute. Finally watched because of RHOSLC and kinda understanding why Meredith didn’t recall the plot no shade.
I think we could’ve cut the drone shots, but this was a great post-Thanksgiving view. Jake Gyllenhaal is so scary sometimes (complimentary) — what a rush!
This was better than I expected; less family holiday dramedy and more thoughtful drama. The story of Akira Emoto’s character hit me the hardest. I want him to have his own movie!
Great for a lazy morning, where you can wander in and out of the plot without much consequence. Not bad, but not terribly enticing.

Netflix release a theatrically filmed feature in theaters challenge. Looks great, but would’ve looked better on a big screen. And maybe that would’ve allowed me to overlook the simplicity of the plot.
Cute, and very chaotic. Deadeye scares me.
Amy Madigan ate down. The rest of this is filler. Tender, but filler.
An appropriately faithful adaptation. Ari and Harriet slipped out a bit much for me, compared to the first part. In any event, glad they did this.

More than a literal triggering to have a villain have my name and then for that name to be said ceaselessly throughout the film. This was fine. At the end of the day, why are you eating a chocolate cake, when you don't like chocolate?

tmdb-425274.title: Now You See Me: Now You Don't

reviews.reviewText: Maybe the worst movie in the series? But also maybe the most enjoyable. I guess I really just like Justice Smith.

They have Glen Powell working overtime to win me over...and it's starting to work. This is not even a good movie, as much as it is a fun romp through a dystopian world that looks all too familiar. With a side of ass.
A bit of a decline here, even though we added Lizzy Caplan. Feels like one film might have been enough, even though we keep trying to cement a franchise series.
Is this the only remains of 5 Pointz? Interesting...

I don't love that Göring is the only character pointing out the danger in the hypocrisy of the other characters. It humanizes and validates him in a way that makes today's world scarier, especially as an American. Here we go again.
Very funny, but the internal logic didn’t hold up and it should’ve been gay. I fear Callum would’ve been my choice.
I've been sleeping on Renata Reinsve. Sheesh! Living for depressed actor older sibling representation. The house as a character is chilling.

The selling point is maybe Oscar Isaac's sexiness or Jacob Elordi's physicality, but it really should be Mia Goth's earnestness. I'm surprised at how much I liked her in this. Guillermo is hit or miss for me, but this ate. I especially appreciated the communication of the weather; felt like I was watching in 4-D.
Honestly, just stuck on how unearned the fantastical element is.
A great lazy Sunday movie, which is to say that it lacks substance, but has people you know in it.
Didn't think this would be bad, but genuinely shocked but how much I liked it. Love when an action film just gets right into it. Alison Janney can do everything?

Films like this are so important because they show how racism can manifest as straight up mental illness. It's not as simple as "someone doesn't like me;" it's as fucked up as "this person wants me dead and to blame me for my own death, at that." I wish some of the stitching of the footage had been more cohesive, but overall appreciated the decision to use bodycam footage to underscore police involvement in perpetuating racism.
I like Laura and Liam just fine, but I could not care less about these characters' whinging.
Great work, but a bit of a slog for my taste, especially being familiar with the source material.

I’ve said it before, and I’ll say it again: strong performances don’t make a good film. The script was contrived to the point of eroding the foundation of the performances. I wish that hadn’t happened because I can see the higher potential in here. Another tick on the con list for having children.
Sorry, no.
Not my favorite entry in the franchise, but the team showed up and did the damn thing!
Not revolutionary, but deeply relatable. The color story is particularly well-executed.
Tender, but I’m asleep. Do we need to making iterations of this film? If so, why not explore other races, at least!

This entry looked the best, but we’re not actually getting any better. They maybe should’ve stuck to the television series. That said, the genre crossover goes kinda hard and Greta Lee is controlling it.
Begging for this franchise to be interesting.
Compared to the world that has been marketed in the back of this film, it falls flatter than expected.
Not a bad movie, but too meandering for me without any payoff. Cannot say this lived up to the hype, but I also get what it is to be in love with a pretty, talented, asshole like DDL’s Woodcock.
I finally get it — bring back fun movies!
Plot was rather obvious, but anything for Ms. Pike.
Loved this, but it just missed sticking the landing. Otherwise, this is exactly what it feels like to be high in public at the wrong time.
Interesting, but not revolutionary. The best work in here is Jeremy Strong’s, unsurprisingly.
Bloated and cheesy box office cash grab. Channing Tatum is still very smashing.
A marked fall from grace that takes away from the world of the original.
Ethan Hawke shouldn’t be this hot. I’m surprised that I found this enjoyable and not just flashy horror slop. There’s a clear line from here through to Weapons.
Jesse Plemons is taking it! Loved the ending. Not sure there was much about this that spoke to me on a deeper level, but I had a good time and it was well made.
There was so much room for this to be interesting, but instead we got three acts of the same story without much additional context or commentary.
Religion is so scary.
Aziz Ansari gets it. Unfortunately for me, this was too real and so I saw a drama when I was trying to go to a comedy.
Tonatiuh’s performance and Diego Luna’s hotness are the only reason why I came and didn’t leave.
Movies used to be so fun!

Unfortunately, I am from central Massachusetts, so I spent the entire film cringing over how it looked nothing like central Massachusetts. Still, a funny and fine film, even if I wanted more Hope Davis.
This wasn’t really good as much as it was enjoyable to watch Ethan Hawke do whatever. Margaret Qualley strikes again…
The ending was a bit over-telegraphed for my liking, but this was fun.

A shallow exploration of the way academic institutions protect white supremacy, particularly when it comes to protecting Black women from sexual harm. Ultimately, this film says very little about its subject matter — and what it does say might be part of the problem it comments on.

tmdb-1160360.title: If I Had Legs I'd Kick You

reviews.reviewText: I was very stressed for Rose Byrne. This film made me question if I’m mentally prepared for parenthood (not that I’m trying to be) — and really, if anyone is or has been recently.
I kept waiting for something to happen and, for whatever reason, I thought there was going to be a sci-fi fantasy element. I was disappointed on two counts, but boy is DDL fine!
The Rock was great and Emily Blunt was underutilized. Enjoyable, and also a tough watch. The scariest part is the relationship between Mark and Dawn continuing for so long.
Everyone showed up! Regina and Benicio were particularly captivating to me. The entire thing is a relentless tour de force that does not feel nearly as long as it is. Can that road win any awards?

They got me girls — I’m ok the Austin Butler train. I wish this had gotten even wilder or had better pacing through its wild moments. It would be rated higher, but the misogynoir and lackadaisical storytelling sandbag it before it gets going.
The promise of Brandy’s return to horror filmmaking was not fulfilled.
Haters of this film are largely without vision and without humor and with intellect. Maybe a rare case, but this film is so “low art” that it becomes “high art” with resonance.

The whole angle of the film is so woke that only one ending is ever viable, so one might hope that the ensuing time would be spent creating some sort of meaningful commentary, but alas. I enjoyed the character relationships, but this is ultimately more of a character study than a film.

Cheers to both of them because I’m not sure I would have survived the events of this film. Everyone in here is doing good work, per usual — nothing revolutionary, but this is the difference between a more diversely skilled ensemble and one where everyone can hold their own.
James Sweeney is a sicko (complimentary)! I wanted more faggy Dylan O’Brien, but overall his performance of the mentioned twins was really quite sweet and beautiful.
Haunting behavior, but also deeply human in its pursuit for connection. The last act, in particular, may have broken me a bit.
Aubrey and Chris are interesting in this, but underutilized. And J may need to accept that I’m just not a Margaret Qualley fan.

I finally get the hype. They really don’t make them like this anymore. Yes, it’s a rather hokey plot, but it’s so committed to its story and its characters without even being camp — it just is itself really well!
I love Riz, but this is just fine. I’m not clear why Rachel was such a big part of promo for what is effectively a cameo, but sure!
I did consider leaving the theater. A bunch of people I like, all in different movies.

An exceptionally well-partitioned and well-sequenced horror film. Austin Abrams really showed up, to the point that I thought his character was someone they weren’t. And we live for a June Diane Raphael jump scare!

Ultimately, they stuck the landing. The franchise itself isn’t “great film,” but they made a really delightful and meaningful sequel, which seems rare these days. Obviously, Manny Jacinto deserved more screen time.
This had no right to be this good. Almost too much so, in that it began to cross genres.
Moving and beautiful to observe. I love architecture!

A romp of a movie. The pace and action make it more enjoyable than its plot or script do on their own. And the cinematography matches that pace and action. These sick white men are unfortunately very hot?
Not really a stunning movie, but I really enjoyed being a film bro dude guy for a few hours.
I expected more anti-Zionism than I got. And deserved more Milly Alcock.
Stressful. I’ll have another! A good reminder of the values inherent in being single and the sacrifice inherent in being partnered. There’s also a sort of pro trans angle somewhere in there.
Molly Gordon is truly scary sometimes. Logan Lerman didn’t deserve that! Will be thinking about those strawberries for a while.
Had all the pieces, but not the tools to assemble itself.
Harrowing and heartbreaking and hilarious and heartwarming. I’m still not a cat person, but I get it.

Somehow I got all the way into my seat before realizing this is Marvel. No shade, but I’m just not a big comics person. I came for the actors, all of whom I love and all of whom delivered. Vanessa Kirby was truly the standout and I cannot wait to see where she takes this character in subsequent work. And while I love Ebon, it should be noted that his The Thing feels a lot like cousin…
I was triggered and on the edge of my seat the whole time. The satire is so well-executed because it’s so close to reality. I’m coming around to Austin Butler, but Pedro and Joaquin are why I came.
I wanted this to draw me in more, but I was truly bored the whole time.
I guess so?
I’m not clear on why this was so bad when the first one was more compelling than expected.

I didn’t go into this expecting an award-winning film. That would be contrary to where the franchise currently is. But this was better than expected, in no small part due to Jonathan Bailey and his ability to have chemistry with anyone. But what would have been so wrong with them just being friends? And him being the gay man that he is? Let me know! I’m also not here for mutant dinosaurs — a step too far.
Ironically, this felt like it pulled punches she never would have. Maybe that’s a lesson learned.
At the end of the day, the vibes of this were giving minstrelsy. I’m not saying the performances weren’t good, but who was this for, after the black squares of the summer prior to this release?

tmdb-713776.title: If Anything Happens I Love You

reviews.reviewText: Oh, that was a bit of a doozy. Simple, but real — and we love a King Princess feature.
I will watch anything for Adam Scott, but that doesn’t make it quality!
Important story, but told with nothing to say in its heavy-handedness. Lots of iconic actors in here, though!
They’re all annoying and I only care about the alleged records.
What a romp! Love the backdrop of hospitality against what is Taken, but about a pet. Nic Cage is such a chameleon. Would be curious to see this character in his heyday.

I probably would’ve liked this more if I watched it when it came out. Viewing it now, it seems so shallow in its discussion. Of all the unhoused people to care about, what about the many Black and brown people? Or do we only care when the people look like Frances McDormand?
Felt a bit like they smashed a few too many plots together. Not sure this is really memorable, in the end.

I became a fan of Kojima playing Metal Gear Solid 4, growing up. The long cut scenes that made me feel like I was controlling the events of a movie had a lasting impression on me. That said, even when the focus of a documentary, he remains enigmatic. It’s enough to make you wonder if it’s all just good PR. Well-deserved, but PR nonetheless, perhaps. This was maybe just more PR for Death Stranding — not mad at that.
Not a great documentary necessarily, but I appreciate us continuing to evidence that rich, white men must be stopped.
PTA is gonna do it every time!
Ah yes, the trauma porn of the black square era.
A serious improvement from her previous entry, but that’s not saying much I fear!

tmdb-660297.title: Ilana Glazer: The Planet Is Burning

reviews.reviewText: When this first came out, I could never finish it. And now that I have, I see why.
To me, this is what the Aliens franchise hoped for itself. Both terrifying and incredibly funny, I couldn’t look away. And maybe Kurt Russell was part of that, but so what?

I’m so fascinated by what was going on in here. Why are the vampires actually rather benevolent? Why are we not just saying the younger brother is gay? Is the mom really giving wellness? Love the creative approaches to demonstrating the power of the vampires, though — that was very cute.

tmdb-1468108.title: Matteo Lane: The Al Dente Special

reviews.reviewText: A lot of nuance that goes left unexplored, which has the effect of allowing the viewer to cast Matteo Lane as somehow more problematic than the average person. The most interesting part is the ending, which feels more like Lane walking through an open than making a stage exit. I so wish we had gotten to see more of what was on the other side. But I suppose mission accomplished given his self-identified brunch catchup style.
Been seeing Jodie go through a lot on my screens this week and this might be her actual best recent work. That said, this feels like so much intensity to not really say as much as I’d hope.
An important history, but this was not really gripping at any point.

Too much of the action is driven by saving a cat. Like I get it, but be serious. The backstory to the franchise is interesting to watch play out, but doesn’t necessarily make this an interesting entry.
Definitely some funny moments, but I don’t care about these characters and their whiny, white problems.
Not bad, just not impactful. And probably not terribly memorable, with the exception of the $33,000 therapy refund request.

There have to be ways to set period pieces that don’t rely on slurs to demonstrate the time. I wish the last act had been the second one and that we could’ve seen these characters experience a bit more integration (pun not intended).

I’m not saying we shouldn’t have emotionally earnest zombie movies. But I am saying that this entry hasn’t perfected the craft. We spend way too much time in a world lacking earnestness to enjoy what they get to. And then that energy becomes squandered with an ending that reads as an entry point into a more interesting film that was teased from this one’s prologue. Give me Jimmy and his Teletubby gang going bonkers for 2 hours! All love to Jodie Comer and Ralph Fiennes, though. And I guess ATJ was nice to look at, but what a shite character.

tmdb-1266321.title: Inside the Mind of a Dog

reviews.reviewText: A little fluffy, but I appreciate being affirmed in my love of canines. I wish this had been more educational, but it almost spent too much time showing the world of dog research, rather than sharing information.
The globalization of American alt-right values and propaganda is terrifying. And in the contemporary moment, this film feels so critical. I want to be Jacinda Ardern’s friend — she is so cool.

Good, but mostly felt like Succession B-sides. And while no one is redeemable, it would’ve been more interesting to address the actual differences in how they’re irredeemable. Hint: it’s age and race!
The horror vibe was so present I forgot this was a comedy more than once. Still, deeply funny and somehow very endearing.

A real sharp entry into the visual album genre established by her elder collaborator. The visuals are not necessarily astounding on their own, but they execute on the job of elevating and legitimizing the album. I haven’t put it down since watching.
At a certain point this felt like camp, but then we introduced sexual assault and really didn’t deal with it well? Still, made me question if I’m about marriage or love — something to consider.

The most frightening question really is: what happens when we die? I love that the film basically starts there and eases us into: how would you live if you knew just about how imminent your death were? The second act was so beautiful and precious!

I was nauseous the entire time. The story was incredibly well-plotted and the chilling effect was well-earned, as a result. The analog vibes of it all really had me even more on edge and I think we should unpack why that is.

There is so much richness in this entry from Anderson. I can’t wait to watch it again. It feels like a more appropriate follow-up to his work in The French Dispatch than Asteroid City. There is more gelling between the script and its cast. Benicio del Toro should be getting nominated left, right, and center for his performance.
I don’t think it ever has to be this serious. I really need people to listen to their siblings.

If you don’t understand cinema, that is your problem. This had the perfect balance of action and gravitas and, to be honest, I loved every cheesy moment. However, it does feel like they have more in the tank, so I won’t feel any type of way if they announce another entry in a few years.
I was kicking my feet and giggling the entire time. Tim Robinson gets it.
I’m not clear that I’ll ever be a fan of Scarlett.
Not as high as he was making this film, but good to know he can still sing. Why are Jenna and Barry involved in this?
This is a spiritual successor to Bullet Train or at least is in the same universe. And I’m here for that!
This was great — everything you want out of action film. Many people are just anti-Black is the thing.
Kinda wish they would turn this into series to explore more of the endings and opportunities available to the characters in the game. That doesn’t make this good — just enjoyable.
I was anxious for him the whole time — thanks! Why are men so weak and evil?
Enjoyable, but not revolutionary. Aesthetic isn’t material content when deployed this way.
Proof that Theo James can’t quite just do whatever he wants.
I feel like we needed more time with these characters, but it was tender and cute all the same.

Cinephiles will overrate this film just because it’s Cronenberg. It was great to get some with Vincent Cassel, but his commitment to playing technocrats is getting irresponsible. This character drives a Tesla and that’s really all you need to know about this tale of grief.
A lot of meh going around, but Andrew Garfield is brilliant. Commentary is lacking, but we take it at face value.
We’re really just out here greenlighting anything. We couldn’t get a final season of Westworld, but we can continue to get trite content like this.

tmdb-1401905.title: Don't Die: The Man Who Wants to Live Forever

reviews.reviewText: Chilling because he’s just ethical enough to only do this to himself, for the most part. But even this is positioned to elide over the fact that the EVOO gives Elizabeth Holmes vibes. Truly bizarre and haunting vibes throughout.
I need the tension ratcheted up a bit more. I see where the foundation was, but the dial needed some cranking. I was pleasantly surprised to see Tommy Dewey on my screen!

I don’t know where I was when this first came out, but boy was this striking. This might be my preferred Andrew Garfield performance and Michael Shannon ate. Laura Dern was a nice little treat, too. Fuck the government!
So bad it’s good. I don’t make the rules, but she makes the web.
Yikes? Nice to see old releases in theaters, but I prefer them to feel like a revelation when I do, especially on first time view. Bill Murray is dark-sided.
This was niche content made just for me. You try putting on Shakespeare, let alone inside of a video game built for incels.
I’m upset it took me this long to see this, but I’m glad I finally did. I was stressed and horny the whole time, a perfect simulation of everyday life to celebrate 4/20.
Love this take on Cinderella. They sort of double twist that creates an origin story for one of the stepsisters was cute. Cindy was always that girl, though!
Naomi Watts was cute and this felt surprisingly grounded in its emotion. The dog was the real star, obviously.

There’s a lot packed in here, but much of it feels forced. The script does not have a ton of continuity and the cinematography doesn’t make up for that. The ending cheats out narratively, and in a big way. But boy is Michael B. Jordan fine and I do love an Irish vampire. Welcome back, Cookie! Still processing that Hallie Steinfeld is mixed.
Solid camp performance, across the board — pun intended. I continue to believe people are sleeping on Fred Hechinger, but that’s their problem, not mine.
Tim Key was annoying as hell, but also the only one with any legitimate perspective, so do with that what you will.
White men are so insufferable because why exactly do you have a panic disorder? I needed more Maya Erskine.

A solid movie that is a love letter to Oakland as much as it is a vignette-based romp through a bunch of Nazi killing. We love! Jay Ellis and Pedro Pascal. Need I say more? And then Normani is here, maybe explaining why she wasn’t in the stu.

The horses weren’t that swift and I don’t think the conclusion was earned. The twin stories spend too much time apart and we don’t get enough of Will Poulter, although his assets are more than appreciated. This pitches itself as a 5-hander (?), but in reality it is a mishandled reference to Brokeback Mountain from start to finish.
The aesthetic is great and it’s a perfectly fine movie, clocking in at a reasonable length. Nice to see Mr. Goggins.
Karen Soni is so boyfriend. Every time I found myself resistant, they managed to resolve my concerns. I’m not sure it really made a ton of sense, but I love when love wins!
Surprisingly fine. Nothing more enjoyable than Rami Malek being a little freak, but not sure that it’s acting!
This was honestly incredibly overwhelming. The scale, the design, the particular commentary on who gets to live, die, and be saved. Boars only become more frightening to me the older I get.

This film was captivating start to finish. Its pacing was inviting while still being strategic and the script had just enough opacity to require the viewer to string together what is going on by themselves. And we all know I love this being lensed through tennis. An interesting project for Naomi Osaka to be part of the producing team on.

Chilling, sexy, and hilarious, but I did want it to be a little darker in the main plot. It felt a little too bright in its construction and the main character being trapped did not feel as claustrophobic as I had hoped. I live for all the small town characterizations.

I love Natalie Dormer and Jimmi Simpson, so that helps. I appreciate that the actual core thesis of this film might be that patriarchy blocks societal progress while its contents focus on a biographical retelling of how scientific progress in our understanding of cancer was achieved, at a certain point in time. By no means is this a revolutionary film, but it also need not be. I was pleasantly surprised at the romantic undercurrent that the film architects but does not comment on until the credits have nearly arrived. Because I was most certainly rooting for Audrey and Dan (apologies to his ex-wife). The corporate politics of it all were also not lost on me. There is a Ronald McDonald house just a block away from where I saw this. Food for thought.

tmdb-648579.title: The Unbearable Weight of Massive Talent

reviews.reviewText: Like I guess?

There’s a lot of good in here, but it feels like a sketch or proof of concept, rather than a finished work. At a certain point, why not include full length songs and visuals from the delude album version and still have it be a short film? That said, I feel like she is the most in tune with her emotions that we’ve ever seen her in this.
Not this penguin having me crying. Vivian El Jaber was clocked in.

A straightforward tearjerker, but well-acted. The whole audience was crying throughout the last act. And I haven’t had that experience in a theater in a long while. It’s nice to see Barbie doing well and we love Lolo!

Deeply hilarious and a simultaneously self-aware satire, this film surprised me with how much I liked it. The performances were in alignment and the monster design was beyond my imagination. The ending was perfectly ambiguous and I love that for us. Téa Leona is so mother.

Not mad, but not a revolutionary or interesting entry into the mafia movie canon. The last 15 minutes are pretty good, but you know it’s rough when it must be acknowledged that the best part was D**** M******.

This was such a wild ride. I think the questions the film poses about the dual relationship between the privilege to becoming a parent and the responsibility of being one are interesting. Its handling of intersectionality is a bit sloppy, but it manages to at least effectively demonstrate the complexity. I wish it had taken more creative license with the script pacing; while the core setup dictates 7 days of “assessment,” it would’ve been more interesting to not see what feels like every moment of that 7 days. Minnie Driver’s character was the only one clocking tea.
This was incredibly trite. Might as well make this review “black bag.”

There’s a lot to chew on and a lot to like in this film, even if it’s sometimes a bit contrived. There’s really smart theory going on and it feels like the epilogue just starts to break past that into a more interesting story. The cast really is all my faves, but Ayo definitely shines and people could learn from her mastery of tonal neutrality that affords so many reads of single moment. The only piece that didn’t really sell was Malkovich as a former pop star — or at least the quality of music production, thanks to The-Dream, was only suitable for true pop stars, not actors. I still love him, though; the acting was otherwise top tier, as usual. Ultimately, I wanted more exploration of the central thesis, rather than the flashy mystery horror this was.

I think there was a slight pacing issue to this one, but that’s nothing to say of the script’s and performances’ strengths. Community, family, rituals, and morals collide every day and this is one of the better examinations I’ve seen of that collision. I appreciate that it leads up to, but does not provide a solution. Its particular area of focus is so under-examined that the work itself is a solution, in many ways.

This has more gravitas than expected, but not enough to make it something I would consider prestige. And there’s nothing wrong with that. But I do wonder if additional casting budget would’ve led to a different outcome. In a lot of ways, this gave worst nightmare. I was stressed the whole time, especially when Simu Liu was involved.
What the hell? Sure.

In truth, I thought this was going to be more psychological thriller than drama. That aside, I felt very emotional watching theatermaking being given a spotlight. I appreciate that the film wanted to tackle trauma and abuse and how it tracks through the industry, but I don’t know that it said anything revolutionary or particularly insightful. Seyfried continues to prove she is a tour de force somewhat cursed with mid projects.

I think this is mostly a really great Harry Potter fanfic with Robert Pattinson trading up from and referencing his role as Cedric Diggory. This was very enjoyable and funny, but also at times haunting. I don’t know that it found its satirical tone; at the very least, the tone is from the President Kamala Harris timeline. And something about that feels unnerving and dangerous at a time like this. Also, can we stop shoehorning every other type of story into a romance? This would’ve been much more enjoyable if Naomi Ackie’s Nasha had stronger motivations that didn’t come back to her relationship with Mickey.

This was life-changing to me. I grew up on Prince’s sounds, but not really his visuals. Insofar as that’s true, his cult of personality was definitely crafted around his sexiness and sexuality, but I truly underestimated the levels at which those both operate. I’ve never been sadder and hornier in a theater and I’ve never been so earnestly moved by such a campy work. Seeing this has recontextualized so much of my favorite work for the better.

tmdb-1278263.title: The Seed of the Sacred Fig

reviews.reviewText: Human and harrowing in its exploration of political and civil unrest, this film pushes to have us understand the impacts of political theatre and secrecy. Its timely arrival, interwoven with real-life footage, is a harbinger of the global times we find ourselves in.

I was more overstimulated than engaged. There’s a lot take in here, but the shape of the delivery makes it challenging to process. Artistically, I understand the goals of the production and editing choices, but I don’t think the balance of those choices was appropriate to a documentary feature of this length. It came across as esoteric, when it didn’t need to.

Chilling insight into a country and culture know today for its quality of life and attitudes toward the safety of children. The middle act was exceptionally tender and I wish we could’ve stayed in that time of the film longer.

It feels like the prompt was: turn Succession into a movie, but with no dialogue and they’re all animals! Highly enjoyable, but the dog slander is wild to me when the rudest one was the damn capybara. Fuck them birds, too!
This was a ride. Trippy and emotional, it suffered from a somewhat stifled first act that somehow catapulted itself into a gripping third act that had me reaching for the tissues.

There is great value in this work. However, I have also written several works that ought to have been several sessions. I feel for everyone involved, but I’m not sure that this film achieved its goal because it was too caught up in expressing its anger to properly channel its wide variety of emotion.
Admittedly, I wanted more grappling with the surviving family’s concept of justice. However, as expected, this only just barely scratched the surface on that narrative.

Timely and well-produced, the existence of this film feels like nothing short of a miracle. While I am wildly impressed by the production, though, it is the story focus that is most critical. I often ask if a work of art needs to be made and, if so, right now. Typically the desired answer is “yes,” and that is, unfortunately the case here. I would much prefer otherwise, but this film meets the reality of today.

I, too, am a traumatized child musician whose initial instructors liked to blend stern chastisement with effusive support. This is a simple film, but it does well to depict the complexity of uncontrolled emotion in youth, especially as it pertains to art-making.

The most interesting thing going on here is Jeremy Strong’s character study of Roy Cohn, which doesn’t say much considering its proximity to his portrayal of Kendall Roy that bleeds in more than once. The film itself cannot decide if it wants to be a biopic, a satire, or a satirical biopic. This is most reflected in the transition from Sebastian Stan’s 45 raping Ivana into the final act. The result is a muddled portrait of a would-be American president that frequently seems to glorify the poor behavior that has led to our poor reality as American citizens.
It just feels like we’re not pushing hard enough on accountability in this narrative. The treatment is more that of a mystery, which — while fair — is not my preferred angle.
A proper TNT film. Nothing was handled appropriately or well and post-production obviously had no budget. But who cares?! I’ll be thinking about boba straw as lethal weapon for a while.

An amazing story that is so focused on the particular torture of a justice system that works as it is designed, which is to say to not provide justice. While the inciting incident of this film is a rape, I admire Shiori for documenting the Japanese justice system, rather than herself; that awareness made all the difference here. In many way, she de-centers her case in composing the work and that sacrifice paid off, as her bravery did in her case. Meticulous and daring journalism and I hope we’ve not the seen the last from her, in that regard.
A TP movie I didn’t hate! Make some noise for Shanice Shantay, for real.

tmdb-1195310.title: The Only Girl in the Orchestra

reviews.reviewText: Another flat documentary about an interesting music star.
So hot that she seduced her own brother and a cardinal? And she’s a lowkey genius? Love that for her!

I thought the monkey would be responsible for wreaking way more havoc than he was. In reality, this is just a slightly spooky movie about deranged and estranged twin. Thankful for an Adam Scott sighting, though.
Sadly shallow for such a complex human being.
There should have been more Ennui and Envy, but you didn’t hear it from me. Why do I love Embarrassment so much?
I see everyone hyping up the hot dad is ignoring that he’s a bum!

Military propaganda at a time like this? I wish Natalie Morales had been given more, but she used what she was given very effectively. I have [redacted] things to say about Ed Harris. There should’ve been more interrogation of his life as a white man among Black women, especially with the history of military service that the film centers on.
Men need help. And I’m not sure that can be found in a hair transplant in Turkey.
I’m not certain that I am a fan of whatever was going on in here. The stop motion animation didn’t help.
Sweet and universal with a warm ending. To be young again!

tmdb-1156619.title: In the Shadow of the Cypress

reviews.reviewText: This was incredibly moving and conveyed so much with just its animation. The style was brilliant and the story was well-developed. I’ll be thinking about that whale for a while.
This was very cute, but definitely seemed like two proofs of concept mashed together. The animation style also gives me the creeps.
We didn’t need to put a young Black girl through that, even if she acted her face off.
I would’ve liked this more if it hadn’t been centered on a white woman. Still, a necessary entry for these times. Fuck ICE.
Hilarious and timely. I think was well-contained and well-produced. I could’ve watched a feature version. Do you think you would know if you were a robot?

tmdb-1279229.title: The Man Who Could Not Remain Silent

reviews.reviewText: I appreciate the sentiment that all it takes is one voice, but we didn’t actually take that commentary any further.

A star rating hardly seems justified, but I do want to somehow mark the quality of this film. Putting that aside, I was completely immersed in the story of Basel, Yuval, their family, and their community. There is so much tenderness amidst the terror. It truly makes one wonder about the resilience of the human spirit and the violent ugliness of humanity. This film demands attention and should be required reading for these times. Free Palestine!

The Sister Act foreshadowing went hard. This was so fun and silly. I appreciate the reckless abandon with which they regarded continuity. I get why this is such iconic reference, and also the game of telephone that has been played with said reference is beyond. Hyperrealism is serious. How did I just realize Whoopi doesn’t have eyebrows?
Irish Hatfield’s and McCoy’s with no expansion on the formula. All the men were hot, but added nothing the situation. Much like life.
We love a woke biopic. I wanted this to delve more into the darker themes of the story, but I also understand why it didn’t. Better than expected, in the end.

This feels like it needed a bit more time to wrap up, even as a short. I hope that this was just a sketch and that the story gets further developed into something more robust and full. I love the sisterhood at the center, but I yearned to know so much more.
There’s an angle on this that is just The Substance for men. Definitely a well-made film, but more shallow in its narrative than I had hoped, considering the topic.
This film boldly asks, “What if Emily in Paris were a horror film?” And I think that’s great.

There was so much texture and care in every element of this film. I immediately felt like I was living in the world of the characters. Much of that has to do with it being history that is somewhat known to me, but I think there is a larger consideration for communicating feeling that is what is crying out for attention every second of this work. And that feeling is mostly those of a mother and wife, in that order. There’ll be a Reubens-sized hole in my heart for a bit.

The chemistry between Keke and SZA is the star. There’s every bit of fun with all the realness of true friendship, especially in a trying situation. The plot was unafraid to be sort of random, which I appreciate in a film designed like this. I love Black people. We’re so hot, funny, and talented.

This was surprisingly more cohesive than the trailer suggested it would be. They were more than a few interesting shots, but overall the script wasn’t as interesting as the story and the end result reflected that. That said, we love a cheeky young woman who’s not afraid to catch flights, not feelings.

I’m mostly here for Sophie Thatcher supremacy, but seeing the rest of the cast felt like seeing a cult classic form in real time. That said, this felt like Westworld or Dollhouse for dummies. The first two acts are grounded in relative reality that the third act feels like it’s cheating out. It waits until the last minute to throw serious theatrics and the cohesion of the film’s narrative pays the price for it.
There was so much space for this to be more entertaining, but despite the stakes of the plot, the film never quite gets there. The middle act was pretty cute, but they didn’t stick the landing.
A weak story and flat visuals.

I would’ve liked it more if the mechanics of the eponymous presence had been more consistent. Ultimately, I’m here for a chilling thriller that operates through classic mystery storytelling; I love how many strings get pulled together (and drawn loose), throughout the film. It’s kind of amazing that Chris was the only one with any sense.
Whew. I wanted to throw up the entire time. I loved it! This story is so specific, yet so relatable to anyone that grew up outside of the majority. Why are kids so mean?

Why is my face wet? And I’m literally asking because I think is a very well-executed children’s movie. And there’s nothing wrong with that, but it’s all style with minimal substance that somehow still manages to elicit emotional response. I don’t even like animals or tech like that for real.

A great reminder of why art that is necessary and urgent is necessary and urgent. I would have preferred that this film avoided “nigga” discourse. Thankfully, I was too preoccupied with wondering how many Black men would be given permission to have emotion after this film to worry too much about woke politics. Theatermaking is such a big part of me and this was the realest representation of what it can look like. And I saw the realest version of me reflected in the men of this story.

I appreciate that they had to state that Adrien Brody’s character was ugly right from the beginning because they know how hot he is. This film was hornier than anticipated and that was the first sign. Putting that aside, Brody gives a world class performance that made me forget he was acting. The scale and vision demonstrated throughout the film really helps to build up the stakes of the story and had me ready to watch for another 3.5 hours at least! I don’t know if its stance on Israel is as clear as maybe it needs to be, given the participants in the making of this film. That said, the film isn’t so much about that and I think that what it is about is so much more broadly human and chilling: what is the price of our dignity, pride, and success and where do those costs intersect?

The world is burning and Almodóvar does not want to stick around to watch. And I get that. The blocking and cinematography felt engaged and the performances felt perfectly “normal,” by which I mean the characters were having incredibly normal borderline emotionless responses to a traumatic series of events. Where the emotion seeps out is so much more powerful to witness because of that.

That was painful to watch (complimentary). At once deeply triggering and profoundly insightful, this film felt like studying someone I’ve known forever. I know Pansy so well, and I think that’s what so beautifully heartbreaking about this. Because J know that it’s not just Pansy, but it’s everyone in her life that want to be close to and love her that are affected as well.

The second act was some of the cuntiest work I’ve ever seen (complimentary). However, overall the gesture felt less grand than its display. Everything felt too close; some distance would’ve served the work, in several ways.

I don’t always think people are cast to act. And usually, I am frustrated by that. Here, it works. There is so much verisimilitude between Pamela Anderson and her character that it seems like no one else could be cast. Jamie Lee Curtis’s performance is also one of her better ones, in my opinion; of course, her performance in the post-film Q&A has distracted from that. I love that Ms. Shipman can eat up a quick 8-count! Justice for women, at the end of the day.

This is actually incredibly close to perfect. I didn’t realize it was musical, and I would’ve preferred it as a drama with music, but it worked! Up until the treatment of the last two songs, and the ending narrative, I think this was really quite special and original. The choreography went hard and the fits were fitting! I’m not an overnight Robbie Williams fan, but I’ll be damned if I’m not enamored with this film.

Mostly fascinated by Ms. Saldaña deciding she’s Black again. For a musical, I’m not sure how much music there really was. And the storytelling, especially the choreographic storytelling, was not compelling. I am shocked that Karla Sofía Gascón could pull the level of performance out of this script that she could — good for her.

I wish they had taken the scrapped the first half and expanded the second half, slowing down a bit to savor some of the plot points more deeply. The second half breathes life into yet another biopic centered on the romantic life of its star. The rich commentary on the folk music scene, competing cults of personality (especially where money-making is involved), and the pursuit of self-actualization by way of freedom all feel more lived in within the portions of this movie starting at 1965 and going onward. That transition might be my favorite: Chalamet gives a certain vibe akin to Beyoncé in her music video for “Jealous” and I love that. Ultimately, this late stage richness isn’t enough to overwrite the lingering on Dylan’s love life in the first half, especially given his general apathy toward all things that love him. I’m impressed at Chalamet’s vocals; which is surprisingly unsurprising — he’s had quality there if not just misused in the Wonka. I do wish the background story of race and civil rights had been brought more into the foreground, instead of the ham-fisted acknowledgments. That said, a better biopic than I’ve maybe ever seen.
What’s always confusing about her is that I can never fully give her my full attention. Yet, I am in stitches when I do. I can’t quite put my finger on it, but I want more and I hope we get it soon!

It’s hard to put into words the feeling of finally seeing yourself in a film shaped like this. The below is an attempt and nothing more. The use of perspective is what hooked me from the beginning. Paired with the choreography of the shots, in some ways this felt like the closest I’ve seen film get to capturing theater. And I wonder if viewpoints, in particular, were a creative resource for that. I would normally say it’s problematic for a white audience to be forced into a perspective that positions them as the main character of a Black narrative, but I wonder if this film actually even does that. Is it actually that non-Black audiences are implicated as bystanders? In this sense, the film reminds me of Get Out, although the execution is far more visceral here. This film feels academic, yet raw and unvarnished; studied, but not didactic. If anything, I think it’s far funnier in places than the audience I was in the theater with understood. I can imagine this will be syndicated for an incredibly long time, especially for educational purposes. Those purposes though, are related to both form and function. Specifically, for teaching both film and Black American history and contemporary culture. I am so grateful a film like this exists. While I could have benefitted from it years ago, it’s just as healing and freeing to me now, which suggests the longevity it possesses is great.
We deserved better handling of kink, which is mostly to say a better third act. I know that intimacy coordinator was paid, though!
Why am I h-word in the theater? We went more style over substance with this one, but I’m not that upset about it, to be honest.

tmdb-1358820.title: A Nonsense Christmas with Sabrina Carpenter

reviews.reviewText: She’s so cute and funny I love this girliepop. But this was not good?
I’m not even really certain I saw this thing with my full eyes. Was Fran Kranz even there?
I knew what this would be and I was still offended. Glen Powell isn’t even that attractive to be doing all this, for real.
I kinda see why Ms. Zelleger won the Oscar; I kinda don’t. Justice for Judy.
I felt so dearly for Gloria. She just wants to be loved out loud. I get that. She’ll always have her dancing and her community.
I’m always gonna support Ms. Shipka. I like that this was romantic, but also decidedly platonic. Caleb Hearon is a show stealer.
I think the tension was weirdly overdone for me. It went in too long, without any releases and, while that may be the point, I’m not sure I liked it.
What was really going on?
I want to live in this castle. I want Howl’s attire. And I want a tighter plot structure.
I question if anyone in this movie had any sense. I wouldn’t have taken the high road if I had been her.
This is incredibly sad, but that doesn’t make this a good movie. The performances feel like a collection of vignettes more than anything else. And why exactly is Troye Sivan here?
This is peak anti-AI tech illiteracy. We are so fucking cooked.
War movies are not my thing, but all these actors are. That said, everything felt stunted and the movie dragged on.
I could’ve gone for a couple more tunes, but otherwise hard to complain about mother.
No part of me realized what this was about, until it was too late. I thoroughly enjoyed myself, though! The rap game is very serious.

Angelina, we are ready for you to come back. This film is interesting starting place for that. On the one hand, an incredibly interesting person to make a film about; on the other hand, such a specific one that one wonders why not make another documentary, specifically.
This is the type of situation only a white man could survive.
I feel like I kept waiting for plot points to be more fully tied together. Instead, it just kept going and getting worse for everyone involved.
I don’t love a slice of life centered on a narrative that feels overdone, but here we are. There’s lots of real in this performance, though, and I appreciate that. Even if it pains me to look at it.
I was hoping this would keep up the momentum from prior installments, but it feels like it’s flatlined into chaotic mess. It was fun, but at what cost?
I do have the opinion that this is for gays and not faggots. Alas, I am beginning to understand the Drew Starkey craze. Why was Jason Schwartzman in a fat suit?

I was so excited for this and then, by the time it had finished, I wondered if I had just sat through hours of Zionist propaganda. Trying to put that aside, this was a fairly strong film.
