The pacing felt urgent and purposeful, and I wonder if this event shouldn’t be studied in management trainings for various roles. John Magaro was unafraid to eat.

To get this one, I think you have to be willing to accept that it’s also produced like films of the era it is laughing at. And it’s asking us to laugh with, in that regard. It was clever and funny, throughout, and a good first entry from Kyle Mooney.
Maybe I’m a hater, but I simply don’t get it. Why the musical number?
What is it about this bear?
With love, this began to expose that Kieran Culkin maybe just plays himself.

I was questioning how this was so well-received, yet so sleepy. And then I realized it’s a Clint Eastwood film. And it’s no shade, but he’s inconsistent and this could’ve been a single episode of any random procedural. In fact, I feel like I’ve seen it.

A shockingly strong adaptation. Ariana Grande needs every award immediately. Some of the flourishes I didn’t love, but on the whole I am very in awe of the demonstrated talent of the entire team that put this together. Still, nothing justifies that “Defying Gravity” battle cry.

Denzel Washington should be billed as a lead actor for this. To me, this was the rare sequel that outpaces its predecessor by miles. It is a 5-star movie, but some of the elements that make it so also make it a lesser film. I think I’ll be rewatching this the rest of my life just for the Shakespearean masterclass given by Washington. Fred Hechinger’s performance won’t be spoken about enough, but it is what it is.

Just an excuse for white men to acknowledge they go to prison too? Weird and uncalled for levels and styles of violence. Perhaps the only thing of note is how networked the characters are. That felt real.
I was more interested in this than I thought I’d be. However, it really is just another flat sci fi action film with some sort of omniscient big bad.
I was waiting for this to get weirder. By the end it almost got there, but a lot of time was wasted being a more “thinking,” rather than “feeling” body of work.
This is a reminder that some people still need this type of film. Not bad, just very angled at a cis audience that isn’t the actual focus of the narrative.
I came here for Peter Dinklage.
Maya Erskine forever. This was a bit cheesy, but it’s less than a straightforward romantic comedy. I appreciate that there were a few glimmers of the reality of catching feelings unexpectedly.
I’m not sure what I expected. But I guess about this…which is basic and catered to boring white men that don’t actually face this much adversity. Thanks, Pete.

I thought this would be better. And I’m here to celebrate Aubrey Plaza as a star, but I’d rather her do fewer projects and have them be smashes. Instead, we get the odd one like this that doesn’t quite hit. The fear of love and losing narrative is so boring to me. I would rather see a film that encourages loving as hard as we can, instead of resisting it. But maybe that wouldn’t have produced as much tension.
This film’s title actually is more descriptive than I realized. And I should’ve known that.
Shocked to report that I didn’t care.
What a romp! Even though I would have liked to see more polish, this energy is what I need more of from Sean Baker.
The concept of time as a literal currency is interesting enough. But this film did nothing with it, other than give dystopian steampunk aesthetics.
I had never heard of this movie. Upon watching, I understand why.

I was on the edge of my seat the entire time, but then there was no payoff. It feels like Sean Baker avoided the even more violent reality of this sort of story. It’s maybe only realistic because it’s dealing with whiteness. And I can’t really let that go.

I believe in Sophie Thatcher supremacy. That doesn’t make this a good movie. I wish this had been spookier and more philosophical, regarding the religion angle at the center of the work. In the end, it feels like it only dealt with the more literal premise of impressionable youth.
She’s better than me because I simply would have passed away. It’s frightening to me that a film like this doesn’t feel at all far off from our reality.

This was an interesting approach to a prequel. I need a third installment to go even further back and explain how we got started. While the series continues to be interesting, it feels like the surface of the lore is just being scratched.

I was locked in almost immediately. As someone with a fear of heights, I cannot believe people’s aspiration to live at the top. But this was a solid take at the problematic outcomes that result from an anti-community mindset. I think my food of choice would be a baked mac and cheese (no bullshit breadcrumbs). Whether or not it would get to me…is a different question.
Actually terrifying, but not necessarily a good horror movie.

tmdb-1371206.title: Megan Thee Stallion: In Her Words

reviews.reviewText: I gotta support the girl! I was surprised at how much depth there was in this for a celebrity documentary. The sad reality is that she’s been through enough to make such a documentary more viable at this career stage than it otherwise would be.
The majority of this movie pissed me off. Its navigation of racial politic seems pedestrian. By the end, I was laughing enough to care less about that.
I endured more than I should for Margo Martindale.

The hype machine got to this one and I guess that’s okay. In many ways, how I feel about this is aligned with my shock at seeing the rating attached to the trailer. It doesn’t feel like the stakes were ever really that high and if we just wanted campy Catholic aesthetics, then I think those could have been pushed further. For all the heavy hitters in this film, it feels like they never stepped into the ring. And the central conceit of the film was delivered in such a heavy-handed manner and too late into the narrative. In the end, I didn’t care.
This feels like a fever dream. And not in a good way.

I wanted to like this more because Bryan Cranston always does it, but this never really got going for me. It’s solidly produced, just nothing special in the genre of undercover drug bust historical fictionalizations.
This took a while to cook. Once it got going, it was a very enjoyable time tale and the mystery of solving it was just difficult enough. It’s as if The Paperboy were more sci fi.
In which this series continues its swan dive. This was slightly more interesting than its immediate predecessor, but it ultimately wasn’t memorable for me.
This felt like really poorly done trans commentary. And even that may be giving it too much credit.

Watching this right after the more contemporary version was hard. You can see all the source text, but you can also see all the technological advancements that have happened since this one was made. While it’s an interesting story nonetheless, this feels roughly executed.

It took me a second to get here, but this is the year of my learning to like Luca Guadagnino. This was chilling and campy in a way that made me shocked to perceive Dakota Johnson as interesting to watch. I’m happy for that.

I’m not sure how I’ve never seen this. The fact that it’s a Clint Eastwood movie made me feel some type of way. That said, I get it know. This is such a ride and Ms. Swank really did the dash! I was standing up and screaming at some scenes.

What a romp! A full fight from start to finish and I enjoyed every bit. This isn’t the world’s best film, but I appreciate its commentary on these trying times where the rich really don’t give a fuck!

Mads forever. This film is a better handling of misinformation than I’ve seen. Even as someone who feels strongly about this issue and the film’s specific events, though, I wonder if films about this topic need to exist right now. The ending was the most chilling bit of it all.
I was crying two seconds in. But then what followed felt exploitative and the fact that the movie itself commented on that didn’t fix the problem.
The best work in this is by Da’Vine Joy Randolph and she’s not even seen. Jake Gyllenhaal looks hot until you realize it’s copaganda and the character sucks at his job.

Something about this is giving unrealized trans commentary. And that’s a step too far for what this cast was giving. The script felt contrived as well, which didn’t help. And to think it’s about the seriousness of EDs.
This was saccharine and sweet, but certainly not good. I’m just glad to see Lindsay back.
I’m predisposed to liking weird things, but this was just bad. Why this story? Why these people? Why this particular vocal work from Evan Rachel Wood?
The tone of this was never really worked out. It’s funny because this feels like a training ground for McKay’s style, which I love in several of his later works.

One of the nuttier things about this is that I thought she went to jail much more recently. I walked away with an even deeper love for her than I already had because like so what if she did a little insider trading? She was that girl! Men do this shit every day with no consequence and I wish the film had said more about that. I appreciate that she kept it controlled and I appreciated the wisdom she shared about changing.

Mark Ruffalo is for the people! I appreciated the pacing of this and how it set the audience up to understand what is a pretty niche execution of a crime. That said, where is the equivalent film about Flint?
The minute I realized Jared Leto was in this, I stopped caring. But it should also be noted that it’s just a boring film that looks good, anyway.

It was interesting to watch this right after the fictionalized version. It’s nuts to hear more context on how someone could get away with such heinous crime, but it all comes down to systemic privilege and systematic failures. This is a fine documentary, but it weirdly doesn’t push enough of an agenda for me.
I go back and forth on how much I like Jessica Chastain’s work, but I do think she’s consistent. Eddie Redmayne pulled me in more than he usually does, which is sad to say given his character.
This felt like it had nothing to say. Even if you take it as slice of life, the slice was stale.
There was space for this to be so much more interesting. And instead, they made it a slot of melodrama.
This was stunningly bad in its attempt to comment on sexual desire and the nature of attraction.

One of the more disarming bits about this is how it handles, but doesn’t handle the undesired sexual tension between the characters. I’m surprised at how emotionally grounded it felt, given this. I probably would’ve taken Sean Penn upstairs after that ride.
Obviously Carrie Coon and Jude Law are not bad actors, but this felt one note for both of them because the story really couldn’t escape the basics: this couple should be married.

This was chilling, largely because it’s real life. Ultimately, I wish it had pushed through some of the narrative more quickly and extended some juicier bits. A lot of this film’s success is really just because it’s a true story.
I liked the energy, but I actually didn’t need this to be a meta musical. One meta scene would’ve been enough for me.
I struggled with this one, mostly because I cannot actually envision Adam Driver being a bus driver.
The second act is great, but after a slow first act and before a third act that tank’s everything it built up.
I wanted to like this so much more because of who is involved. In the end, I think it was quite the romp but ultimately a very boring take on sisterhood and sex addiction.

This surprised me! It was much more interesting than I thought it would be and served as great commentary on the function of art and community, separately and together. The marketing team was so chaotic and I love that.
I’m not clear on why this was so bad. Maybe I just don’t like watching train wrecks.

I’m not really sure why, but I put off watching this one for so long. In the end, it was alright. Andy Samberg is a better dramatic actor than he’s given credit for and Rashida Jones was good, as always. The story itself just fell flat for me.
Sometime I forget how much Meryl has been in. It’s easy to forget that some of these films are straight up boring, even if she’s acting her face off.
I love Paul, but this was too slow without enough payoff.

tmdb-1323299.title: Fanatical: The Catfishing of Tegan and Sara

reviews.reviewText: I do not think that was a good documentary, but boy was I locked in. I think it tries to neutrally complain about the state of celebrity within culture, but ends up being a vacuous complaint about being relatively successful celebrities. I wouldn’t be surprised if another documentary comes out in a couple decades about the actual workings of the band’s act.
This felt like a lazy double-down on the original.
I didn’t expect to like that as much as I did. Very artful and funny, while also deeply disturbing.
Jesse Plemons as a whiny f*****? I’m in! The script felt sleepy, but it was nice to see him and Molly Shannon work together and she was so earnest in this performance.

Watching this now is a reminder of how far we’ve come in 15 years. Part of my is shocked that this film could’ve been released in 2009, and then I think about it. I wonder how Jim and Ewan look back on this movie.
I want better for Dakota Fanning and this isn’t it. Honestly, the reveal was boring and that’s my biggest issue.

I think this film needed to be made, but I’m not sure that Kristen Wiig should have been the one to do it. Much of it is real, triggering, and tone deaf at the same time. I honestly couldn’t look away.

I don’t know what possessed me to put this in because most people involved have irritated me in the years since this was released. And upon watching, I have to say I should have avoided it. It wants to be so legitimate and serious so badly and just comes off as a story about a bunch of petulant adults that never really learn their lessons.

I remember all the hype when this first came out, so I expected it to be more polished and less indie in its feel. That said, I found the content resonant if not a bit whiny at times. Ultimately, I’d watch this again just because Michaela Watkins is in it.
I always love a weird Tilda film, but this felt nearly pointless. It was fun to watch, though. And that counts for something!
This was something I watched…Kristen Wiig is so good, I’m not sure why she ever did work like this.

This was better than I thought it would be, in the end. I can’t say it’s revolutionary, but it was my way in to better understanding the scandal because I did not observe it that closely, or from this angle, in real time.

She needs to be stopped. It’s so bizarre to watch this with distance from the context in which it was released and the context in which its events are set. This is probably a film that didn’t need to be made, let alone released.

This actually feels like source material for Saltburn and it seems like not too many people have noticed that. It’s dark, sexy, and heart-wrenching and should be required pre-reading for anyone wanting to make queer film, given its exceptional quality.
Well…white women talking. And I’ll leave it at that.
So weird. And so heartbreaking. I wanted something more to happen, but it’s really just off kilter slice of life. Bless Noomi, though.
This is very of its time in its delivery. Viewing it for the first time now, it feels very telegraphic of James France as a person.
I just feel like this didn’t give anything extraordinary. I realize this film exists within s subgenre of child revenge on adult, but did it have to feel so much like that?
Love Cate and Rooney, but this was just fine. And Lord knows I’m not a fan of period pieces, so I guess that’s that on that.

This was better than I thought it would be. I look forward to seeing Brittany O’Grady do more work. The story of this film could have been really simplistic, but there are enough twists and turns to elevate its narrative out to the realm of being basic. The dramatic center is also not there just to drive the horror; in many ways, it is the horror, which gives the film a certain gravitas that narrowly avoids being too self-serious.

It’s very easy to make a mid horror movie. The direction in this sets it up to be a prestige horror film. The act structure was well-deployed in a way that enhanced the story, rather than cheapening it. I can see myself coming back to this one and picking additional things up on a rewatch.

I love Mr. Tucci and Ms. Shipka, but this really gave A Quiet Place parody. All of the interesting seeds they plant for the monster of it all, is thrown out the window to weakly comment on human monsters. They really should have picked a lane and stayed in it.

This was so bad. And mostly because it went nowhere. Every moment was telegraphed and the story really offered no takeaway. At a certain point, kinda feels like it’s anti-kink, even though it makes a point to say it’s not…?

This got better as it went. I appreciated the resonance of an academic being taken advantage of by the system. It’s so true that you can be the kindest person, but be regarded as cruel by the world, simply because you’re real and not nice for the sake of being nice.

The curse of a third act that should’ve been the first rears its head. I so wish we could’ve explored the larger world more or not have seen it at all. The small world the film starts in is great, but the world it reveals is, unfortunately, much more interesting than what it actually spends its time building. Ultimately, there’s not a ton that’s memorable in this one.

There’s a lot to chew on, here — no pun intended. The story is well-crafted commentary, but I feel like I just wanted more of the chaos and less of the cute ending out of this. I wanted to see Paul Dano and Jake Gyllenhaal at war with Tilda Swinton. All in all, not a bad take on the American food complex.

tmdb-1039773.title: The American Society of Magical Negroes

reviews.reviewText: I’m not sure that the payoff was worth watching the nonsense of the majority of the movie. I am a pretty big fan of several of the involved people, but this sent the wrong message.

This annoyed me so bad. All this non-plot just to kill the Black man that wanted to mind his own business? I don’t think so, honey! Sydney Sweeney’s character didn’t deserve to be the one who got justice for him. In fact, all of the voyeurs should have been punished more. This was way more sad and not very sexy.

This one took a while to get cooking, but it was satisfying to watch the pot. I appreciate that they subverted what I expected would be a disappointing ending, but the final button was too commercial and nearly undid that work. Julianne Moore and Sebastian Stan continued to deliver and Justice Smith’s range blew me away yet again.

This was cute, but it feels like we just wanted a buddy movie to put George Clooney and Brad Pitt in. And in reality, we needed more Amy Ryan. I had fun, though I wish the ending hadn’t been so rushed; it feels like they spent too much time establishing the leads’ rapport and then didn’t have enough time to leverage it well in the story.

Rather shocked at how bad this was. I honestly wonder if they even meant for it to be good. Each of the lead actors was in their own movie, so it was never totally cohesive. As a result, the output felt stilted and unsexy, which seems like it should be difficult for a murder mystery set in Greece to be.
Yes, Christina Ricci, go all the way off! This film feels both of and ahead of its time. Makes me want to get back into therry real bad.
I tried to find intention in how this film was moving, to justify how bad it is. I told myself that it was camp. But it was not.

Start running Demi her awards now. This was an all gas, no brakes kind of film and I love that for us. I appreciate that there’s no redemption for anyone, but damn are the youth evil. It’s difficult to capture in words the feelings I felt while watching, often all at the same time…but here’s a list in no particular order: disgust, anger, sadness, fear, arousal, glee.
Having never seen this, I’m genuinely shocked by how much more contemporary media pulls from this. I also finally understand the attraction to Joaquin Phoenix.
I think I’m too pilled on the aesthetic Wes Anderson has found since this. As a result, this felt rather dull and without much memorable to even comment on.

tmdb-10313.title: The Men Who Stare at Goats

reviews.reviewText: This pissed me off on so many levels. I didn’t care about a damn thing going on, at any point, and I also had to suffer Kevin Spacey. Please!

The narrative was thin and the cinematography was thinner. I so badly wanted this to have any sense of purpose. Unfortunately, it falls into the bucket of work that doesn’t clarify that it needed to be made.

If there is one thing Willem Dafoe is gonna do, it’s deliver a captivating performance. However, his one in this film cannot save the film from seeming without point or import. What gravitas was built was surrendered in the last 30 seconds, the film’s ending cheating itself out of saying something to recall. A good film cannot simply evoke a mood and offer no commentary on said vibe.

After a nearly perfect first three acts, the fourth act pissed me off; the fifth act won be back sevenfold. That said, the comeuppance of the film’s antagonists was just shy of filtering the tone in a way that made it feel like the film appropriately covered its own subject matter. In the end, it might have suffered this fate due to just being too long. Either that or it needed to be a touch longer.
By no means is this a good movie, but Jake looks great. The last fight sequence was OD. Why is there a lowkey love story going on? You can’t make this one make sense. And that’s okay.
Daniel Espinosa is sick. The last five minutes were particularly effective, although it’s hard to not look at this as imitative of Alien. I suppose there’s nothing wrong with that, in the end.

I slept on this one, but that’s better than being a hater. Unfortunately, I think this film is only designed for the audience it is satirizing. This might be Jake’s best role and I appreciate Toni’s presence. Can’t Help Myself was top of mind.
Noooo Rapace slayed, but there’s not enough substance for me to care about the movie beyond the gimmick of one actor playing seven siblings.

I’ve said it before and I’ll say it again: a good film isn’t about all of the pieces being good; it’s about good assembly of all of the pieces. There is every reason to be drawn in and intrigued by this beautiful, weird, and chilling work. Unfortunately, it feels like it lacks cohesion more than it feels intentionally crafted to cause one to think they are hallucinating.
There’s nothing of substance going on here, unfortunately. Not the worst thing I’ve seen, but also not memorable.
I’m not the target audience, but boy was that exceptionally bad. I hope Joey King moves on to better work because this isn’t it.
I just couldn’t get into this for some reason. It’s not bad by any means, but it doesn’t even feel like there’s much to critique.

Only Carrie Coon is able to get under my skin so effectively. I appreciate  Elizabeth Olson’s presence, but she really felt like a catalyst for the interactions between Coon and Natasha Lyonne. I’m a film about nothing and everything, all three pull together an engaging performance of the final days of a head of household. The script earnestly deals with the complexity of status that comes with all that and avoids cheapening any of it, even in the end. Let the girl smoke her weed wherever, for fuck’s sake!

I didn’t fully realize what this was about, but it felt like a natural progression for Rachel Sennott’s acting career. I appreciated that it felt grounded, but the third act unveiled the reality that maybe it never was. ACAB.
I’m here for James McAvoy’s body, but let’s not call this a good film. Louise was insufferable, so hats off to Mackenzie Davis, I guess. Any was the real star of the show, in the end.

I couldn’t look away, even though I wasn’t sure what I was looking at for most of this one. The stories were wonderfully offbeat and absurd, generating fun and interesting performances. That said, the bottom line is that a film has to be more than engaging to be great. All the elements of a great film are present, but Lanthimos continues to miss the mark on assembly. Given his weakness, in that regard, I thought the triptych setup would help. I fear it only made the weakness more obvious. Willem and Jesse should’ve kissed more.

This had been on my list for a while, but the sadly didn’t live up to the hype I had in my head. It was visually interesting to watch, but it didn’t feel like the story was pulling me in. I look forward to future work from Trace because I know this is just scratching the surface of her talent.

I don’t know what’s going on, but I don’t like it. Terrible plot, terrible cinematography, halfway decent acting, and Glenn Close as a white woman so appropriator-coded that I thought the character may be Black after all. But why any of that?

tmdb-466272.title: Once Upon a Time... in Hollywood

reviews.reviewText: Weirdly, Austin Butler was the most interesting part of this. I can’t quite make sense of what Tarantino was trying to do here. For me, it just seems like a tired Western about the events leading up to a historic moment of infamy. However, the Manson Family isn’t even the focus of the film, so it winds up feeling ham-fisted and misdirected.

A spiritual 5-star film in that it’s fun and engaging, throughout. However, one cannot in good faith say that it’s a film of quality. In the wake of incredibly real hypersurveillance, especially at concerts, the story seems grounded in reality. However, none of the characters interact with a real world. The dialogue is often hyperbolic and borderline nonsensical, giving a sense of caricature to the entire plot. It’s rather unfortunate, considering the music is a bop. And isn’t that what this film is a vehicle for, anyway? Cheers to Josh Hartnett’s performance, though — uncannily wicked, especially in the last few minutes.

Everything in this film is given consideration and that is almost immediately noticeable. I clocked my rating on this at maybe 10 minutes in and then just let myself enjoy the ride. Every moment had me jumping out of my seat or vocalizing into the void between me and the rest of the audience. Zoë Kravitz is clearly studied in her approach; a great joy of this film is catching all the references. From the visuals to the music, there is an abundance to chew on. Never mind how all these actors were assembled to bring together the story. Adria Arjona’s performance, in particular, was quite the standout. The one critique I have is that the trigger warning maybe needed to be given for more audiences; the excess of substance is critical to the movie, and enough to make one vomit, crave their next bender, or both. That said, I’m already dying to see this again. It’s safe to say that I was having a good time.

This is some of Leo’s best work, but I think it’s the function of a script that doesn’t require him to say many lines. I’m most intrigued by him having a Pawnee son. Is whiteness the only way to have films made about Native people? Killers of the Flower Moon is really just a sequel.

Not sure how I avoided this one so long, but I do know it’s because the film makes me literally nauseous. In this case, it’s the sign of good work. I think the story they told could’ve been done in 2/3 the time, but the last act had me up and on the edge of my seat. The Titanic of it all was not lost on me.

The twist was incredibly telegraphed, though maybe I just had it spoiled in the deep parts of my brain after all these years. I find both Scorsese and DiCaprio to be hit or miss and this was no exception. The best part was honestly Patricia Clarkson in the cave.

I’m sort of shocked this more or less lived up to the hype. For a true crime, bordering on heist film, it was incredibly executed. The script is tight and considered, the cinematography is sharp, and the acting isn’t half bad. Nothing revolutionary occurs, but it was nice to see these women all together, despite my knowledge of more recent events regarding several of them.
This was cute and wholesome. Nothing radical is happening, but it was enjoyable to watch while working. I’m always gonna support Sandra and Jason.
Fun, but of its time. Part of me feels like we deserve an updated take with Meg and Flo Milli. But maybe that’s just me.

Wack white crime. They couldn’t even be bothered to amp up the editing to make this interesting. And it’s not that crime should be “interesting” to hold our attention; it’s that nothing in what’s presented does the job of documentary to lead the viewer to a conclusion. I literally was so disinterested that I couldn’t tell you whose side I should be on. A giant miss.

tmdb-595813.title: Barb & Star Go to Vista Del Mar

reviews.reviewText: I slept on this one for too long. It’s an earnest romp of a film that finally convinces me that Jamie Dornan is anything more than average. The chemistry between Kristen Wiig and Annie Mumolo is electric. This is definitely one I can see myself regularly putting on as a mood booster.

A chilling look at early reality television. It’s both hard to look at and hard to look away from, and it’s difficult to observe its questions as feeling answered or resolved. Our obsession with voyeurism and celebrity-making is rotting us, but are any of us better for knowing that if we still participate?

There are some really stellar moments in this. The entire second act is a delight. Ultimately, though, it’s so long and chaotic that it becomes difficult to pursue finishing. The use of various actors was curious. I would never do Amy Ryan wrong; she scares me too much.

Bodies, Bodies, Bodies in space. And to be honest, that pissed me off. The stakes didn’t feel appropriate to this group, making it feel unimportant to the world the franchise has set up. The technical elements are spectacular, but serve an undeserving narrative.
The use of Native people felt cheap. Their being Indigenous felt relevant to the characters, but not to the plot, which threw me off. Naru is that girl, though!
Another one I didn’t care about. Weirdly it felt like it belonged to the decade prior to the one it’s of. Lots of humor, but not in a way that gives the franchise any legitimacy.

Not as strong as its predecessor. Still quite strong. I can always use more Bully Crudup, even when he’s playing the world’s must stupid character. Speaking of, none of these characters being able to tell the difference between Walter and David is nuts, even if they’re both played by Michael Fassbender. Be serious!

The highest rated entry in the franchise, post-Predator. And it makes sense. This felt like Ridley Scott’s Arrival and I didn’t mind that. David is a little shit; Michael Fassbender’s acting is top tier in this role. And Noomi Rapace’s work was haunting, chilling, and stunning.
I didn’t care what was going on in this one at any moment, even with Adrien Brody on the screen. Consider that my guidance on this one.
Similar to its predecessor, this film is incredibly of its time. The nostalgic feeling isn’t enough to make it an actually good film, but it does make it enjoyable to watch.
I didn’t see this link up coming. Not a world class film, but good in a nostalgic way. Movies of this time were really just out there doing whatever. Often in pyramids.
Still not meeting the original’s quality, but Ripley’s character development is a slay and Sigourney Weaver is hot as acid. Literally.
No? I like the ragtag bunch vibe of this one, but it wasn’t holding my attention. The stakes weren’t high enough for me to care about the ending, which is bizarre, considering its implications.
Really flat and disappointing, given the franchise’s starting point. Weirdly this one was not War propaganda enough.

The start of this film was too war propaganda for me. But the ending had me shifting like tectonic plates. The last 45 minutes are perfectly camp and so horny that they made me think Arnold Schwarzenegger was hot for the first time ever. I don’t really even care what this movie was about, at this point.

James Cameron effect went hard and paid off. Something about this wasn’t trying so hard as its predecessor, which made it better. I do think the relationship between Ripley and Newt is off, though. Let me say that.
Nothing extraordinary, despite what rabid fans will tell you. definitely an enjoyable space film moment, but not terribly memorable, even though the franchise is seemingly ubiquitous.

I’m afraid the hype train got to this one. It’s visually interesting and the acting isn’t horrible. However, the actual story and script are so bad, it’s difficult to know exactly how strong the other elements of the film are. Rooting for Hunter always, though.

The first two acts created e beautiful crime thriller with elements of magic just to chalk it all up to magic in its last act. While everything was considered, down to the directional scroll of the end credits, I couldn’t shake the feeling that it tanked itself. Nonetheless, i had a good time and could see myself watching this many times in the future.

Usually, I want less exposition and explanation, but this needed more. This film was beautifully shot and had me engaged the whole way through. However, there was no pay off because it lacked in critical story detail that would have given its plot a sense of gravitas.

I love an “illusion of choice” moment, but this was executed in a confusing manner. The stakes are where they should be, throughout, but unfortunately they’re never earned. It’s even brought up several times that these characters don’t know each other well enough for everything to be happening like this and they try to magic that away with the plot, but it doesn’t really work. And then they also just drop John Slattery’s character from the film halfway through. Apparently, I am trying to go through Emily Blunt’s entire filmography.
Lots of stars, but not enough going on to actually be intriguing. I imagine for the time this is quite good, though.
This was better than I thought it’d be, but not by much. I’m glad Rian Johnson left this genre to Christopher Nolan. I much prefer his mystery work. Shoutout Emily Blunt, though!
I like Joey King more than I realized. She’s the only interesting thing going on in this movie, but it is nice to see Nicole Kidman and Zac Efron reunited.
This had a few moments that were surprisingly engaging. I want to like Dakota Johnson more as an actor. The end was unexpectedly tender and I appreciated that.

As always, i am on the right side of history. Federer was one of my gateways into tennis fandom and the record reflects why. Sadly, this documentary feels somewhat hollow for such a revered person and legacy. The most interesting thing is maybe processing how recent his retirement is in the grand scheme of things.

I bawled like a baby. Celine is so special and this documentary feels surprisingly open, but not bereft of her typical levity. I’m so glad to have her share what part of her journey she can with us. This film is, in part, an indictment on the job it is to be a real, true celebrity entertainer. And you won’t get that if you don’t get it already.
I can’t help but feel that this was closer to performance art than standup. Nevertheless, Hannah Einbinder is a clear talent. Once she warms up, it’s nothing but laughs.
Cute and darkly funny. This is a great movie for smartly crafted stupid laughs. The burnout thinks really did it for me. And Henry. Justice for Henry.
While I was angry the entire time I watched, something about the last 30 seconds convinces me that it will age well as an iconic moment for the girls who are just going with the vibes.

Always going to root for Sonya Walter, but this missed the mark. It feels like this was interesting, but it never knew what story it was telling. By the end, nothing had weight and whatever message could have been there was erased.

It’s always hard to transpose a live performance to film, but NT makes sure to do so consistently. Andrew Scott breathes new life into this classic, updating it while staying true to its core. And all while flitting through emotions, physicality, and facial gesture belonging to so many characters that it makes your mind reel.
I wanted this to be so much better. I appreciate a slow-paced, quiet film, but form must follow function and it doesn’t feel like those techniques served this film. It was nice to look at, though.

Chilling throughout, I was scared to keep watching this film and also couldn’t look away. The plot was suspenseful and the sound and music editing strongly supported the vibe. The button with the Mormon boys might be my favorite part.

Justice for Justice. I’m not sure how he keeps finding himself helming films, but not making more of a name for himself that allows him to helm better ones. This is definitely an alright film, but the pace is too sleepy to the point that it loses its message. Some of the most interesting bits are the film structure. I especially enjoyed the “television show inside a film” bits.

tmdb-653346.title: Kingdom of the Planet of the Apes

reviews.reviewText: A surprisingly good movie about apes. There were plenty of things that I found incongruous, bordering on offensive (the chimps understand and speak English and Sign Language, but speak in broken grammatical?), but I have to admit that the storytelling and cinematography were punching above the film’s weight. Justice for Raka.

tmdb-1059319.title: The Feeling That the Time for Doing Something Has Passed

reviews.reviewText: This was laugh out loud funny, but only for those that understand the vibes. An iconically New York film and also a film that deeply understands what it is to function as a knowledge worker in the upper middle class of American capitalism. Beyond that, a really moving story about understanding one’s self in relation to others and accepting that that understanding evolves over time.

In the end, I think the cast made the movie overhyped. The heavy lifting is done by the plot and the script, and the performances aren’t out of this world. The drama of history was well-shown in the design aspects of the film.

This film is beautifully shot and the script is relatively sparse, which I think they made work. However, it may have been too sparse; I’m not entirely sure how the various threads of the plot are connected or even if there’s a greater point it is trying to articulate.
Too meta for its own good. Too many “names” to affirm its point. I may need Ryan Gosling’s plastics surgeon’s number.
Better than I thought it’d be, but missed major opportunities to care about what it was producing. There are more than the arcana.

Such a hilarious and interesting film. The script is plenty funny and the plot if classic sci-fi while still feeling modern in what it’s curious to explore. The ending doesn’t feel cheap and it really feels like more people would be talking about this if it were a major live action production.

The rare film that made my stomach turn. Beautifully grotesque and chilling until the last second. This one is all gas, no breaks and we should be thankful. I do think I would’ve preferred a situation without a “final girl,” though.

A really sweet film that manages to be deeply funny and completely earnest about the nature of friendship between single people and their partnered friends. I was nostalgic for the feeling of New York community throughout.
This was a nice soft watch, but I don’t think it really has a compelling story. The whole arc is an unjustified romance that is really supposed to be about a difficult childhood.
It’s not clear to me how this plays into the pre-built world from X. If anything, it feels like this is just a separate, slightly worse film.

This one showed more than it told. Which I suppose can be fine…but I didn’t care for it. In the end, I had many more questions than answers and I’m not sure this film was justified in that sort of resolution.

I love Kirsten and Jessie, but I could’ve done without this one. There is ironically not enough clarity to the murkiness of the story’s politics. In trying to offer a commentary, it forgot to set up a reasonable premise on which to build.

There’s a ton of interesting elements in here, but Luca Guadagnino is still trying to figure out how to perfect the formula. In a technically well-made blockbuster, my main critique is something the average audience won’t see: the film didn’t deliver on the promise of tennis. The game is central to the film, but operates as a deus ex machina. At one point, there’s even mention of “true tennis,” but the film never manages to show us that — maybe that’s an intended layer, but it doesn’t feel like it. The set up of tennis in the real world is rife with dramatics, from the game’s rules of decorum to the way the game is shot and televised; the film barely made nods to these elements and, in some spaces, was inaccurate. Tennis as a game and community was owed more in this movie, not the least of which is the opportunity to draw interest from a new audience. Sadly, the film decided this was not its responsibility and ended up missing out significant opportunities, as a result. In the context of a film about a love triangle, it constantly punched down at queerness, instead of offering a commentary on sexuality in sports, especially professional sports. Guadagnino’s obsession with sexuality in the young is something I find unsettling. This film’s script didn’t merit the breadth in its timeline and frequently did not even communicate that timeline well. I had an absolute blast watching Zendaya eat and Josh O’Connor brought the right angle and amount of horny to the work. Mike Faist’s performance feels betrayed by his character’s script and edit. This all said, I’ll probably watch many more times.
Respectfully, I’m good on whatever this is. The only reason it’s not at a lower rating is because of the action sequences.
A cute little moment. I appreciate the glimmers of camp, but overall nothing special.
In the end, I’m just glad this is done. Not bad. Not good. Something I’d take a nap to while it airs on TNT.
James McAvoy is hot, but at what cost? This feels like a gimmick just to bring the franchise (back) into existence. I home the follow-up is better.
Interesting, but not as attention-grabbing as I thought it might be. We love Bruce, though!

There’s so much to chew on in this film — masterful execution of classic techniques, complex emotional performances for miles, and the right blend of terrifying and intriguing that makes me want to see it again immediately…to name a few. While I didn’t care for some of the dialogue at the end of the script, the final tableau nearly sent me into a fit of tears. I’m manifesting another installment in this franchise, whether that be a sequel, prequel, or otherwise. John Krasinski is shaking.
I couldn’t be bothered to stay awake for most of this. There was a lot of good hustle in the last 15 minutes, but enough to change the score when the buzzer sounded.

I wanted this to be better. It’s an enjoyable film to watch, but it falls flat in the end. It doesn’t seem to articulate any opinion on its own plot and then has the nerve to have a protracted third act. There was somehow too little magic for this being magical realism.

There were so many opportunities for this to have a higher rating. I appreciated the plot complexity, but little ended up resolving well or being meaningful. That said, I think the story itself actually made up for a meh script and the acting definitely helped disguise the weak script as well. In many ways, this felt like Jurassic Park with monsters, which I loved, having seen none of the other entries in the franchise. The Skull King was sexy in the same way that Rat is sexy in Fantastic Mr. Fox and, sadly, suffered the same fate. RIP.

I don’t think that gave what it thought it did. Even as a part two, it begs additional work and doesn’t really stand on its own apart from the first installment. Sure, it’s well-directed, but the children saving the day won’t make me forget that they’re the same ones causing all the problems because they can’t follow the rules. I guess I’ll take Emily Blunt and a rugged Cillian Murphy, though.

It feels like this doesn’t hold up. As a first time viewer, I can see where Nolan has grown since this film in handling time-based narratives. It’s not to say it was bad, just that it’s not what the hype would have one believe. While interesting, I think the reverse chronological takes away from the film’s strength, rather than adding to it. The fact that the main character has a memory affliction is enough for the audience to work with that seeing the film in chronological order would’ve actually been more engaging.

Every moment is used wisely. It feels a little lopsided at times, but it resolves itself as much as a movie should while still remaining interesting. Dev Patel has a ton of reasons to be proud. One of my favorite parts of this film was the cinematography and the direction, which is a testament to his overall craft. Well-balanced action and drama that was uniquely sexy and heartbreaking — I’m excited to see how this ages and to rewatch it myself.
There’s a couple people in here I apparently want to go to Paris with. Watch this if you’re looking for a sexy, homoerotic, Nazi film with no opinion beyond colorblind casting.

I appreciate how the film nodded to real life and the book. As ever, Jessica Chastain and Idris Elba gave solid performances. However, there’s not much interesting in the plot to merit its runtime. In fact, I was somehow more interested in the skiing career Molly had before she became a criminal.

So much of this felt over the top and yet unearned. I feel like the most interesting bits were small sequences between two characters, but they didn’t tie any of those moments together. Amy Adams and Julianne Moore seemed underutilized, at best.

I think the challenge with stories like this is making the audience care about the main character even a little bit. And that must be done straight away. Unfortunately, that wasn’t done here so it basically felt like a hate watch the entire time. I’m glad that character got what was coming to them, but it felt more required to the script than natural to the plot.

We love an iconoclast. I love Billie Piper and Gillian Anderson, but they didn’t necessarily give revolutionary performances. I think the script is right and the pacing of the story is crisp, which keeps it entertaining. In the end, it’s really just about valuing the expertise of outsiders.

In the end, lots of poorly executed references. Election years are always curious because they give way to films like this that try to give liberal commentary on bodily autonomy, especially for women, bioethics, and eugenics. It’s not a bad stance to approach from, but if you don’t stick the landing it is painfully obvious. Sydney Sweeney can scream.

I at least had fun, but I think we’re maybe beyind the point of providing queer commentary solely through aesthetics. It leaves the whole product feeling voyeuristic, shallow, or both. What maybe would’ve been an interesting story lost itself in the, more obviously, bad script. Not quite camp, but reaching toward it unintentionally. Zero winking. And while I love dogs, I could not willingly suspend my disbelief that they were effectively a gang. It was giving Oliver & Company. The brother was the most gay-coded in the end.
Not a revolutionary story, script, or set of performances. I still shed a few tears, though.
An experience, really. Mostly just rooting for Gloria and the doctor focused on ventriloquism.
This gave incel vibes artfully. Very tragic and very hard to watch in many aspects. The animation style meets the story well, though.

tmdb-869626.title: Marcel the Shell with Shoes On

reviews.reviewText: So soft and beautiful like a warm hug from a friend you haven’t seen in a long time.
The levels to how terrible this was are shocking. Bad story, bad script, bad acting. Just so much messy chaos and not in a redeemable way.

Proof that good films can be made with relatively low budgets and 90-minute runtimes. Proof that camp and emotional weight can co-exist in film. I’m surprised to be writing that this hit all the right notes: fun, flirty, sexy, criminal, Judith Light, and Audra McDonald. And, maybe even more surprisingly, Lukas Gage continues to prove he has the range. Not only is he one the writers, but he gives a complex performance and his chemistry with Zachary Quinto is enough to make me want to be their third.
It’s hard to see my baby Justin Min being an asshole like this. And, despite them getting to shoot inside Daily Provisions, I’m not convinced this is actually good. What is Tavi Gevinson doing here?

tmdb-1109711.title: Freaknik: The Wildest Party Never Told

reviews.reviewText: There’s a meta level of tension between commerce and community that the film explores and is a part of. However, that doesn’t make up for the material lack of engaging content until its last act. Overall, it might just be a pacing problem, but I think it’s also a problem of the film creators not knowing what the angle of their documentary is.

I was so caught up in the setup that I missed the obvious and I love that. The lampooning of liberalism, wokeness, and the rich by people that inhabit those same classes isn’t lost on me. Still, I live for the levity in the incendiary takes on the social media generation in between the more thrilling moments of tension. Lee Pace feels a bit off in this mix, but go off I guess.

This one has strong concept development and execution, but unfortunately throws most of it away at the end. Rather than leaving things unresolved, it attempts to tie neat bows and reminds you that there are too many things unresolved. Darkly funny, it made me want to be a late night host all the same. We love Gus and a Sarah Paulson impersonation.

Kristen Stewart’s best work and a thrill of a film. You could slice cheese with the dramatic tension and the relief is delivered at all the right moments. The music and sound editing, especially, set this apart and contrast the oft heavy-handed script. At the end of the day, don’t do drugs.

Julio Torres is it, so beautifully playful that he makes me jealous of the world he lives in. He manages to capture a complex and emotional experience, while making different parts relational to people of different experiences. Some parts made me laugh as a human, others as a New Yorker, others still as an artist. It was also great to see legibly queer art that did not center gender or sexuality in its story. If this is his first film, I cannot wait to see what he does in this lifetime.
I loved how camp this was the whole way through. Well-executed editing across disciplines and a very funny script. I don’t know how memorable the film is, but it’s definitely enjoyable.

I think we should be careful about hyping films based on a singular aspect of the work, in this case the visual aspect. For all the value that it has, when the script is lazy it renders the acting as caricature and the film itself as unserious at worst and camp at best. This entry into Villeneueve’s take on the work is a step in the right direction nonetheless. While they don’t make up for the script and performances, the visuals do compensate significantly and are a sight to behold. There’s a richness to the contrasts he sets up between the different “worlds” of the film; they each behave differently and it’s refreshing to see a level of execution that is able to evoke reference and push the medium forward without losing itself. Scarily, this is the first time I’ve found Austin Butler hot. The Bene Gesserit are the scariest of them all, though, really. On a less cheeky note, it should be acknowledged that there’s a level of cognitive dissonance required to watch this film and not think of the several ongoing genocides and the war-state of the world. And maybe that’s the point of making this work now.

This story doesn’t feel effectively told to me and I think that may be because so much is from the dad’s perspective, rather than his daughters. All the same, it’s an important story to have been told.
Oh, they are so back with this one! Hit all the right notes. I think it could’ve been tightened up a bit, but it was a great ride!  We love a modern take on Anastasia.
They’ve fallen back into the generic action movie formula. There’s really nothing special going on here.
It feels like the franchise has finally gotten back to its baseline. Plenty of action and an interesting story. Reminded me a lot of the structure of The Dark Knight Rises.
This feels like it approaches a return to form. It just feels a tad contrived. I didn’t realize Simon Pegg was so key to this franchise.
This is a solid romcom and a pretty bland action flick. I love Michelle Monaghan, but they ain’t slick just getting rid of Thandiwe Newton.
This is camp because they just took the original film’s blueprint and added romance and actors that embody movie stardom to make a subpar sequel. In its own way, ate.

This is kinda the blueprint for action movies, spy-related or otherwise. And the present cheugification of tech has made it so that, at worst, the script is poorly acted at a few points, and at best eerily prescient. At several points, I might’ve thought it was a work of historical fiction if I didn’t know any better.

This is a gay New York ass film. So brilliantly funny while capturing the general melancholy of life and relationships in New York. I still don’t know why we have to racialize animals or why this was set pre-9/11, but here we are.

We live for layers. This film is richly complex and something I will definitely want to rewatch in order to grasp more of the details. In this view, I am mostly in awe of the use of reenactment and “BTS” footage.

I have complex and conflicting feelings about this one. On the one hand, this first account of the Russian war against Ukraine is an indictment on the state of global society and politics. On the other hand, it’s difficult to square the platform and credence this film is being given with the lack thereof for Palestinian people facing genocide in Gaza.

Consistently captivating, but fails to stick the landing. In the end, this maybe had nothing to say as much as it wanted to say a bunch of things aimlessly. I do need to talk to Mr. Liebenwerda, though.
To me, the worst in the franchise. I love Rocket, but it takes too long for this one to gain traction and by that point I’m already bored.
Better than the first one, but really only because of the budget. If you asked me what the plot is, I wouldn’t have much to say.
I guess this movie was fine? It’s unfortunate that Bradley Cooper’s voice acting is attractive.

tmdb-335977.title: Indiana Jones and the Dial of Destiny

reviews.reviewText: Phoebe Waller-Bridge really saved this one. Not good, but not bad…I guess. Nevertheless, I can never really resist Mads Mikkelsen.

tmdb-217.title: Indiana Jones and the Kingdom of the Crystal Skull

reviews.reviewText: This one feels like a mistake. How did they con Cate into being involved? And why are there Mayan inter-dimensional beings?

tmdb-89.title: Indiana Jones and the Last Crusade

reviews.reviewText: HF at his daddiest and caddiest. Spielberg perfected the formula in this one. And he used it better in Jurassic Park.

tmdb-87.title: Indiana Jones and the Temple of Doom

reviews.reviewText: Better than the first one, but also more racist. Still feral for young HF.

Love Mr. Spielberg, but this was overhyped. Quality for its time and I get that, but it’s largely just Indiana Jones being a misogynistic asshole for two hours. And I get why that worked — Harrison Ford is fine as hell. But this doesn’t a good film make.

The final act bumps up the rating after the meandering second act. Honestly, most of the film felt like prologue. There were a multitude of places where effective script cuts could’ve been made and would’ve helped the film. Unfortunately, it was too fast-paced and not well woven together. The end result just felt like trauma porn for the first world.

Do I want to marry Koji Yakusho? Yes, but that’s not the point. I love how this film expounded upon the tension between capitalism and community. You can derive happiness from both, but it is perhaps true that one costs the other. This is an example of how you cover a lot of ground simply and effectively. Every detail is just right and I hope more people watch this in the coming days, weeks, months, and years.
I didn’t have it in me to focus on another country’s governmental decline and popular resistance.
Did we need discrimination in a fake world about elements?
Not much to this one, but boy are these two women goals! I loved getting to know their energy and spirits for a few minutes.
What a sweet tribute to music and a beautiful call to action. I can’t say there’s a ton of pull on this one, but I enjoyed it!
Well-shot, but lacks gravitas. It’s a great porthole into another world, but the commentary falls flat.

You can tell this was produced by The New Yorker, by which I mean that totally it leans more broadcast segment than documentary. And that’s fine, but it does not a good film make. That aside, it’s nice to see a compelling and positive story about Black community be told.
Alas, ye olde propaganda and cigarettes.
It’s a shame that this film needs to be made, but I’m glad it was. It isn’t revolutionary, but it is an exceptional form of resistance.
Beautiful and emotional. The type of love that makes you want to be in love. Suddenly, my eyes are very wet. Pauli and Augusto forever.

I thought this was a Star Wars movie until two days ago. Honestly, pretty flat. Something I’ll fall asleep to on my couch, on a Saturday afternoon in the Fall, when it’s airing on TNT in 2027. Great timing for it to come out, though, with all the AI hype going around.
Cheesy as it is, this film had me crying — until I realized the biographical narrative is contested. Enjoyable to watch, but appropriate to have pause.

This film has only made me like Jon Batiste less and I already had a bad feeling about him. There is a paradox to documentary that often renders the subject too aware for true voyeurism to take place and that’s well and good. However, mitigating that experiential impact isn’t don’t by crafting a documentary in a production style more suited to fictional work. While initially it may seem that the viewer is in store for a rewarding origin story on a misunderstood artist, it becomes apparent that there is no more depth for those paying attention. Contrary to its conceit, Batiste is entirely a performative person, specifically performing for the white gaze. It is evident in the tone and framing of the majority of the film. And, perhaps, that would be expected of the film’s director, Matthew Heineman, given his background as a white man from Darien, Connecticut. Overall, this film is like an overly saccharine and chalky icing on top of a dry, vanilla cake.

“Come with a classic; they come around years later and say it’s a sleeper.” Enjoyable and uniquely intriguing every step of the way, including the historical fiction layer, particularly a vampiric Iron Lady. The choice to stick with black and white was also executed quite effectively, which always pleases me.
Loved the animation style, but I didn’t find the story gripping at all. Really was just here for the gay love story of it all.

Annette Bening and Jodie Foster chewed! Some of my favorite scenes were the psychedelic ones during the swim attempts. However, I think the tone of the flashbacks were not appropriately fit with the cinematography of the rest of the film; the heaviness was not appropriately squared with the lightness, so where contrast was meant to be provided seemed instead like poor aesthetic mix. The story of friendship between Diana and Bonnie is maybe the most compelling. When Bonnie mentions they’ve been friends since they were “like 30,” it gave so much more dimension to the narrative elements on self-actualization. And the village it takes to make it possible for just one.
I could say much more, but the point is really just that I was bored.

tmdb-923939.title: The Wonderful Story of Henry Sugar

reviews.reviewText: Wes ate with this one. I loved the “Russian doll” story. Dev Patel didn’t come to play! One of Anderson’s more fun stories, though I kind of wanted more VFX to add to that feeling.
This one took a lot out of me. Just a boy fighting with himself. It’s sad to see, and too common. I appreciated the care with which this particular story was told.
Darkly funny and emotionally gripping. It’s so hard to capture grief in this way and I think they just about nailed it in this film.

The reveal almost comes too late in this one. The heaviness of the consequences in the lead up isn’t present enough and the twist doesn’t activate it in hindsight. Still, a film that needed making, though it’s a shame that it’s always the child angle we pull on to give weight to dramas like this.

Not a bad film, by any means, but I do think it falls into a common trap for shorts: too general of a message starts to feel too specific and alienates the audience. The end also pissed me off — why take the time to disregard Black male emotion like that.

I want whatever drugs Timothée took to say “yes” to and follow through with this. I just feel like the more we make Willy Wonka the celebrity and leave Charlie behind, the more we stray from God’s grace. Also, like, was he in the stu recording these songs? I must know! And why was half the movie just fat shaming?
Cute and fun, but not much more to it.
This was a slay. I love the animation style, though calling it that feels like a bit of a stretch. Equal parts educational and entertaining.

tmdb-1214020.title: WAR IS OVER! Inspired by the Music of John & Yoko

reviews.reviewText: I feel like the song is enough. Don’t bring my beloved game of chess into this.
This one had a fine start, but the reveal only begged for more. The details would have, perhaps, been better left out. I appreciated the narrative, all the same.
By the time you see the twist coming, it’s too late. Intricately woven and engaging from beginning to end. The mixed emotions of the narrative are incredibly written.
I didn’t love the animation style. However, it grew on me as the story developed. I wish the strength of the ending were consistent throughout the entire film.
A film that states it has nothing to critique, but that also offers little perspective on what it’s narrating. Somehow, even the lack of opinion has no directional opinion.
Once you get past the style, this eats. If anything, makes you want more.
It was good until it wasn’t. Bonus half star for Chuku Modu being someone I want to see in real life.
I see how this led to Uncut Gems and this was better. Ultimately, don’t love the vibes.

Well-made from start to finish. The directing and cinematography are clearly in sync with each other and the plot. However, the plot has little to offer. It starts off strong and manages to go nowhere over nearly two hours. The expressive reactions of other audience members make me wonder if I would have gotten more out of this if I knew less of N*** history.
This was just Tár for men? Bonus half star for the queerness of it all.
This was fine, but I don’t get the hype. I kind of needed more of the cutaway future scenes to put the puzzle together. But it was good for what it is.
This took way too long to get to the twist and didn’t even do it well? Swan Song did it better. All my love to Paul and Saoirse, though.
An extra half a star because I hate men and the last bits really support that thesis. I just wish the whole movie had me in this energy because now I’m UP.

Proof that you can know how to technically craft work perfectly and still miss out on a feeling. If anything, this just didn’t feel messy enough. I also just wish this had been a true three-handed that more deeply examined each of the friends in the core group at the center of the script’s narrative. And speaking of the script, people don’t speak this way. But still enjoyable.

Aunjanue Ellis-Taylor is a force. It’s so rare for me to see a film and come away having learned something, rather than having just been entertained. For all the shelves I’ve seen it on, I haven’t read Caste, but now I’m racing to read it.
What a wild ride. Beautifully leaned and acted. I was sobbing by the end.

Well, this one hurt. And I definitely need to watch it again immediately. Andrew Haigh has come a losing way since Looking, and it shows! There is so much complex thought in here, connecting grief and identity. And who doesn’t love the thought of gay Ghost? If anything, I needed just a bit more, which is not something I usually want from contemporary films.

This was a pretty good film but I think it got a bit lost between being humorous and earnest. In the end, it didn’t have the emotional gravitas to stick its landing. And with a cast of talented and reputable Black actors, I’m certain why we needed to give the With and And credits to the only notable white actors in the movie.

tmdb-399057.title: The Killing of a Sacred Deer

reviews.reviewText: A great watch but didn’t really speak to anything more than what it was. Barry Keoghan being a little freak is my new favorite subgenre. Collin Farrell and Nicole Kidman were a nice bonus.

My biggest regret is that I didn’t see this high. With stunning visuals that feel like an evolution of Miyazaki’s style, this film misses no beats in delivering a classic Miyazaki narrative. At different times hysterical, heartbreaking, and downright creepy, this is one with far more rewatch value than some of its predecessors. Its exploration of the ways in which we seek to order our world, especially through time, is something I’ll be thinking about for a while. Fuck the eponymous heron, to be honest — gave me the willies from the jump. And the parakeets…

I was bawling at the end, no doubt. However, this film comes with all the troubles its predecessors did. This is to say, or rather ask, are there no other Black stories to be told? It’ll never sit right with me that the survivor must always be forgiving to the abuser as a way of establishing a pure morality. That aside, a collection of knockout performances. Fantasia Barrino is singular in her talent.

The only person acting in this film is Penélope Cruz. I’m not sure what I was expecting, but I beg for us to stop giving roles that require accent work to actors not up for the task. Patrick Dempsey being in this made me giggle.
Richard Gere and Laura Linney are both very hot and that’s pretty much the reason to watch this movie.

Love a revenge plot but Ma needed help. Surprised by how many of my favorites are in this. Ultimately, they made a bad movie a camp classic. And really, I’m always here for the subgenre of Octavia Spencer in a horror film (see: Drag Me to Hell).

Marin Ireland’s performance is the one to beat in this ride of a film. It has a bit of a slow start, but the way act makes you want to savor the limited time in the short script. I wanted it to be more gay, but I’ll settle for the Massachusetts vibes and the violent dream state of it all. Anne Hathaway’s scream is the daughter of Meryl’s in Big Little Lies.

I finally get why people think Mark Ruffalo is hot. A great script, though long in some places. I was captivated the majority of the time and found the exploration of personhood, womanhood especially, intriguing. What would we all do differently if we had to re-learn our behaviors again? Admittedly, the one thing that rubbed me wrong was Ramy Youssef dropping the R word 10 minutes in…

Largely, I don’t think this film needed to be made. However, it’s well constructed and I found myself emotional more than once. Maybe it’s my soft spot for medium ugly white boys. A few of my classmates will definitely be getting hard-ons for the good ole days when they watch this.

Oh, yes. In a film full of heavy hitters, the most powerful scene might be one of a final few where Kevin Bacon and Ethan Hawke have a conversation that suggests the end is yet to come. Rising star Myha’la also continues to strike me as someone I want to work with. In many ways, this film feels like it sits in the same world as Sam Esmail’s prior work, Mr. Robot. Both darkly comedic and chillingly real, I was glued to the screen the whole time.

Kinda just here for Julianne Moore’s vocal choices. Charles Melton does give thee performance, but the hype machine is maybe too intent on making this prestige film just because it’s made by Netflix. In another era, this would simply be a well-received Lifetime Original. Natalie Portman’s turn at the end ate, though.

If there is one critique it’s simply that I wanted more. For all of the glory it is to have the tour show itself documented, I found myself thirsty for Beyoncé to peel back the varnish further. This is perhaps the most well-executed show / documentary she’s given us, but real fans know there is so much more: Dubai, fashion house collaborations (especially Balmain and Telfar), Cé Noir, her hair care line, acts ii and iii…her last line has me worried and excited that she’s only just begun and that her foot is firmly planted on our necks (as it should be).
Was this supposed to be a comedy? The only thing saving it was the battle sequences, but even those weren’t top of their class.

Laugh out loud hilarious. It was a certain kind of delight to see this as a surprise screening with a bunch of white people, too scared to leave or laugh for fear of being perceived as racist. Ultimately, I’m on the fence about this as a Black person. Despite its own commentary and internal counterarguments to the main character’s thesis, I’m afraid it might miss the mark and still wind up being Black content for a white audience. I’m not sure what to think of this continued trend I’ve seen in media, and how much I believe my own argument, but I’m keeping an eye on it.

I continue to love Barry Keoghan. What a wicked depiction of the lengths people will go to take what is not rightfully theirs. Beyond that, a fresh take at the payment due for the sin of not being kind. A movie with a striking visual treatment and that I’m certain I’ll be returning to for years. Rosamund Pike was a delight to watch — we’ve all met someone like her. In the end, I didn’t even care who was gay and who wasn’t; it was just a bloody sexy time. And that last scene? Rent free in my head. Points off for a couple continuity errors.
I feel like the hype machine got to this one. Not bad by any means, but didn’t really hold my attention. No one in this can say this is their best work.

I wasn’t expecting to like this one as much as I did and it honestly gave instant classic vibes. Maybe it’s my sentimentality for my private school days, maybe it’s the part of me that likes to be triggered by the same period in my life. My biggest problem with this film is that it falls into the contemporary trap of trying to shoehorn a historically excluded narrative, into a film that otherwise lacks diversity, via its B plot. It’s a terrible crime for a number of reasons, not the least of which is that it often outshines the main story. My one wish for this film is that it had just been about Da’Vine Joy Randolph’s Mary Lamb. l

It’s a shame that so many of the script’s foundational elements mandate that the film be a dark comedy. While I appreciated the darker moments, I wanted them to feel dramatic. I had hoped for an anti-celebrity commentary and instead got an anti-celebrities commentary, which doesn’t feel nearly as appropriate. In fact, that somewhat small inadequacy renders the film disproportionately ineffective in communicating a message worth listening to. Nevertheless, a stunning performance from Nicolas Cage and one I look forward to returning to.

I had high hopes for a Meg Ryan renaissance. While I love airports as a liminal space, this film missed the mark. It couldn’t decide if it wanted to be grounded in realism or magical realism, which is a shame. Had it decided, it might’ve been better executed.

I spent half the movie trying to figure out if Elizabeth Lail was Jennifer Lawrence or Lili Reinhart. That cupcake was doing too much. Somehow, though, felt emotional the whole way through. Should’ve just watched this on Peacock.

A film that perfectly encapsulates what our post-world looks like, particularly for women. A rightful criticism on the lengths we go to to vilify women in order to protect the fragility of the male ego. Sandra Hüller delivers a beautifully complex performance and hopefully this is just the beginning for Milo Machado Graner.
Molly Gordon kinda ate that. Glenn forever.

Scorsese earns more minutes of the running time here than in The Irishman. Lily Gladstone needs an Academy Award immediately for her performance. A deeply moving history and sometimes visual feast, I imagine this will be best to watch in theatres if possible. One of the best parts of the film is how it manages storytelling across timelines, showcasing how generations of abuse have built generations of wealth for the undeserving. In the end, it succeeds in giving voice to the Osage while honoring the truth of a history that would otherwise erase their narrative.
Hilarious and fun. Not a film to see if you don’t know how to laugh. Campaigning for Meg Thee Stallion’s Academy Award starts now!

They wanted this to be Glass Onion so bad! It would’ve been a better film if Tina Fey hadn’t had the world’s most distracting half-assed attempt at accent work going on throughout the entire film — her one liners were kinda eating. It was nice to see Kyle Allen, snack that he is, on the big screen. And, really, who doesn’t like a spritely Kenneth Branagh moving about better than actors half his age or queen Michelle Yeoh stealing the show while being in a fraction of the movie?

It’s always nice when a film doesn’t try to be bigger than itself. By “sticking to the script” so to speak, this relatively straightforward docudrama was elevated. Perhaps one of the more interesting facts about this film is that the Winklevoss twins executive produced it. If one of the principles of the movie and the movement behind it is to give “financial” power to the people, it’s funny and sensible that people seeking to institutionalize crypto bankrolled anti-regulated-exchange propaganda. I wonder how much it pained Pete Davidson to play a character from Massachusetts and whether or not Kate Burton found him charming.

Having a confounding plot twist at the end of a movie doesn’t make it great, even if you’re A24. This one felt like their take on haunted Euphoria — they similarly couldn’t decide here if the story was meant to be ensemble-based or a three-hander with appropriate support or if really there was a single main character. Maybe this struggle was only present because the main character is a Black woman. I’d say “you be the judge,” but you should honestly just skip this one. Be the civilian.

Rachel Sennott and Ayo Edebiri are a match made in heaven. It feels like only they could lead the translation of a script from Emma Seligman and Sennott that is a perfect satire of teen movies that also earnestly delivers on the promise of the genre. For every line toed, several more were crossed and the payoffs were worth it. This is a clear early contender for comedy of the decade and certainly has the staying power to be a long-referenced entry in the genre like Mean Girls (and also like Jennifer’s Body?) before it.

First of all, love a movie with a hot villain. Putting that aside, an exceptional film that mixes elements of horror, thriller, action, drama, and mystery to eke out a narrative that asks the audience to question and re-evaluate its knowledge as the film progresses. In doing so, it manages to earn just about every minute of its nearly two hours, seemingly wringing more time out of itself like one might the water from a sponge or, more aptly, like one might wring the blood out of a bandage.

For all the stars this movie possesses, you’d barely know it because the film’s narrative is ultimately lost in itself, diminishing whatever strong performances that might’ve existed in the raw production — maybe too meta for its own good. As Nolan always is, visually stunning, but not enough to make one care about the world or its characters. Having watched Inception and Tenet before this, it would seem that Nolan treated the former and this film as a parts shop for the latter, taking the best elements of each to make a film better than its predecessors (Make no mistake, this is the weakest of the three.).

A visual and audial feast. Somewhere between being a joy to watch and simply difficult to look away from. A good film, separated from being great only by its lack of cohesive narrative and pacing, each perhaps endemic to the other. Tamara Lawrance is a force that can only make one wonder what this film would’ve looked like with more singular focus on Jennifer Gibbons.
In the end, a typically average rom com, which I suppose we queers deserve. I laughed, I cried. The third act made me want to die. They did Stephen Fry dirty for making him play that dusty ass king.

This film will be studied for a long time. The rare treat that is both enjoyable to watch and feels historic in the moment of viewing. Production notes aside, Greta Gerwig has cemented her place in history with this movie, so aware of its demographic that it’s able to make a genre all its own while also being diligent about even the most minute detail in its story and design. Saccharine and heartbreaking, it pushes past camp to build community live with its audience. Rather than stating in-jokes, it invites the audience to create them in real-time. Above it all, Margot Robbie demonstrates again that she very well may be the actor of her generation. And somehow, in the end, I deeply crave to be in the Mojo Dojo Casa House with Ryan and Simu.

A fun, but lengthy watch. Robert Downey Jr. gives the best performance and Emily Blunt did the thing in that one scene. Cillian Murphy is surprisingly nice to look at. The script could’ve been better edited if it wanted to earn its running time.

Admittedly, slept on this one a bit too long. The rare horror film with strong performances. Yes, Ms. Collette, but lest we forget Ann Dowd and Gabriel Byrne (In Treatment hive, stand up!). Peter deserved so much better, but damn he’s a prince, I guess.
Had some fun moments, but didn’t earn as many of the conceptual bits as i would’ve hoped — speaking of which, Hope Davis forever.

Great Lee and Yoo Teo give stunning performances, but the script could be better. Some of the best moments are in the third act, where there are tons of silence and the familiar navigation of conversation in multiple languages at once.

Watched this again for the first time since it came out. I remembered it being worse, but it’s still not that great. They saved all the juice for the third act and wasted Oscar Isaac’s and Tessa Thompson’s talents.
Great for animation nerds, but I’m not a huge superhero movie person.
The rare film that telegraphs its plot, while still finding the line and then crossing it…multiple times. Horrific and also darkly funny,  but not for the faint of heart or stomach.
